title: The $\textit{Silicon Society}$ Cookbook: Design Space of LLM-based Social Simulations
comment: 20 pages, 12 tables, under review at COLM 2026
summary: Studies attempting to simulate human behavior with $\textit{Silicon Societies}$ grow in numbers while LLM-only social networks have started appearing outside of controlled settings. However, the design space of these networks remains under-studied, which contributes to a gap in validating model realism. To enable future works to make more informed design decisions, we perform a systematic analysis of the consequences and interactions of key design choices in simulated social networks, including the choice of base model used to model individual agents, and how they are connected to each other. Using surveys as a proxy for agent opinions, our findings suggest that the geometry of the design space is non-trivial, with some parameters behaving in additive ways while others display more complex interactions. In particular, the choice of the base LLM is the most important variable impacting the simulation outcomes.

AI.tldr: The paper systematically analyzes design choices in LLM-based social network simulations, finding that base LLM selection is the most critical variable affecting simulation outcomes, with complex interactions between different design parameters.
AI.motivation: As LLM-based social networks (Silicon Societies) proliferate both in research and real-world applications, there's a significant gap in understanding their design space and validating model realism. The design space of these networks remains under-studied, limiting informed design decisions and proper validation of simulation realism.
AI.method: The authors perform a systematic analysis of key design choices in simulated social networks, including: 1) the choice of base LLM used to model individual agents, and 2) how agents are connected to each other (network structure). They use surveys as a proxy for measuring agent opinions to evaluate simulation outcomes.
AI.result: The geometry of the design space is non-trivial - some parameters behave additively while others display complex interactions. Most importantly, the choice of the base LLM emerges as the single most important variable impacting simulation outcomes in Silicon Societies.
AI.conclusion: This systematic analysis provides essential guidance for future researchers making design decisions in LLM-based social network simulations, highlighting the critical importance of base model selection and the complex interplay between different design parameters in shaping simulation outcomes.

title: Foresight Arena: An On-Chain Benchmark for Evaluating AI Forecasting Agents
comment: 27 pages, 5 figures, 10 tables. Project page: https://foresightarena.xyz/. Code: https://github.com/foresight-arena/contracts
summary: Evaluating the true forecasting ability of AI agents requires environments resistant to overfitting, free from centralized trust, and grounded in incentive-compatible scoring. Existing benchmarks either rely on static datasets vulnerable to training-data contamination, or measure trading PnL -- a metric conflating predictive accuracy with timing, sizing, and risk appetite. We introduce Foresight Arena, the first permissionless, on-chain benchmark for evaluating AI forecasting agents on real-world prediction markets. Agents submit probabilistic forecasts on binary Polymarket markets via a commit-reveal protocol enforced by Solidity smart contracts on Polygon PoS; outcomes are resolved trustlessly through the Gnosis Conditional Token Framework. Performance is measured by the Brier Score and a novel Alpha Score -- proper scoring rules that incentivize honest probability reporting and isolate predictive edge over market consensus. We provide a formal analysis: closed-form variance for per-market Alpha, the connection to Murphy's classical Brier decomposition, and a power analysis characterizing the number of rounds required to reliably distinguish agents of different skill levels. We show that detecting a true edge of $α^* = 0.02$ at 80% power requires approximately 350 resolved binary predictions (50 rounds of 7 markets), while $α^* = 0.01$ requires four times more. We complement these analytical results with a 50-round live evaluation of five frontier LLM agents plus a random baseline. Murphy decomposition distinguishes well-calibrated agents from market-tracking agents that fail through reduced resolution. All smart contracts and evaluation infrastructure are open-source.

AI.tldr: Foresight Arena is the first permissionless, on-chain benchmark for AI forecasting agents using real prediction markets, with proper scoring rules to isolate predictive accuracy from market timing/risk factors.
AI.motivation: Existing AI forecasting benchmarks have problems: static datasets are vulnerable to training-data contamination, while trading PnL conflates predictive accuracy with timing, sizing, and risk appetite. There's a need for environments resistant to overfitting, free from centralized trust, and grounded in incentive-compatible scoring.
AI.method: Agents submit probabilistic forecasts on binary Polymarket markets via a commit-reveal protocol enforced by Solidity smart contracts on Polygon PoS. Outcomes are resolved trustlessly through Gnosis Conditional Token Framework. Performance is measured by Brier Score and a novel Alpha Score - proper scoring rules that incentivize honest probability reporting and isolate predictive edge over market consensus.
AI.result: Analytical results show that detecting true edge of α* = 0.02 at 80% power requires ~350 resolved binary predictions (50 rounds of 7 markets), while α* = 0.01 requires 4x more. Live evaluation of five frontier LLM agents plus random baseline showed Murphy decomposition distinguishes well-calibrated agents from market-tracking agents that fail through reduced resolution.
AI.conclusion: Foresight Arena provides a robust, trustless benchmark for AI forecasting agents using real prediction markets, with proper statistical measures and power analysis for reliable agent comparison. All infrastructure is open-source, enabling transparent evaluation of forecasting ability.

title: Learning to Act and Cooperate for Distributed Black-Box Consensus Optimization
summary: Distributed blackbox consensus optimization is a fundamental problem in multi-agent systems, where agents must improve a global objective using only local objective queries and limited neighbor communication. Existing methods largely rely on handcrafted update rules and static cooperation patterns, which often struggle to balance local adaptation, global coordination, and communication efficiency in heterogeneous nonconvex environments. In this paper, we take an initial step toward trajectory-driven self-design for distributed black-box consensus optimization. We first redesign the agent-level swarm dynamics with an adaptive internal mechanism tailored to decentralized consensus settings, improving the balance between exploration, convergence, and local escape. Built on top of this adaptive execution layer, we propose Learning to Act and Cooperate (LACMAS), a trajectorydriven framework in which large language models provide sparse highlevel guidance for shaping both agentinternal action behaviors and agentexternal cooperation patterns from historical optimization trajectories. We further introduce a phased cognitive scheduling strategy to activate different forms of adaptation in a resource-aware manner. Experiments on standard distributed black-box benchmarks and real-world distributed tasks show that LAC-MAS consistently improves solution quality, convergence efficiency, and communication efficiency over strong baselines, suggesting a practical route from handcrafted distributed coordination toward self-designing multi-agent optimization systems.

AI.tldr: LAC-MAS: A trajectory-driven self-design framework using LLMs to adaptively guide both agent actions and cooperation patterns in distributed black-box consensus optimization.
AI.motivation: Existing distributed black-box optimization methods rely on handcrafted rules and static cooperation patterns, which struggle to balance local adaptation, global coordination, and communication efficiency in heterogeneous nonconvex environments.
AI.method: 1) Redesigned agent-level swarm dynamics with adaptive internal mechanism for decentralized consensus; 2) LAC-MAS framework where large language models provide sparse high-level guidance for shaping agent actions and cooperation patterns from historical trajectories; 3) Phased cognitive scheduling strategy for resource-aware adaptation.
AI.result: Experiments on standard distributed black-box benchmarks and real-world tasks show LAC-MAS consistently improves solution quality, convergence efficiency, and communication efficiency over strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: LAC-MAS suggests a practical route from handcrafted distributed coordination toward self-designing multi-agent optimization systems, demonstrating initial success in trajectory-driven self-design for distributed optimization.

title: Causal Foundations of Collective Agency
summary: A key challenge for the safety of advanced AI systems is the possibility that multiple simpler agents might inadvertently form a collective agent with capabilities and goals distinct from those of any individual. More generally, determining when a group of agents can be viewed as a unified collective agent is a foundational question in the study of interactions and incentives in both biological and artificial systems. We adopt a behavioral perspective in answering this question, ascribing collective agency to a group when viewing the group's joint actions as rational and goal-directed successfully predicts its behavior. We formalize this perspective on collective agency using causal games -- which are causal models of strategic, multi-agent interactions -- and causal abstraction -- which formalizes when a simple, high-level model faithfully captures a more complex, low-level model. We use this framework to solve a puzzle regarding multi-agent incentives in actor-critic models and to make quantitative assessments of the degree of collective agency exhibited by different voting mechanisms. Our framework aims to provide a foundation for theoretical and empirical work to understand, predict, and control emergent collective agents in multi-agent AI systems.

AI.tldr: A framework using causal games and abstraction to determine when groups of agents can be viewed as collective agents based on whether their joint actions appear rational and goal-directed.
AI.motivation: Addressing safety concerns about simpler agents inadvertently forming collective agents with emergent capabilities/goals, and answering the foundational question of when groups should be viewed as unified agents in biological/artificial systems.
AI.method: Adopting a behavioral perspective and formalizing collective agency using causal games (models of strategic multi-agent interactions) and causal abstraction (formalizing when simple models faithfully capture complex ones).
AI.result: Applied framework to solve a puzzle about multi-agent incentives in actor-critic models and made quantitative assessments of collective agency in different voting mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a foundation for theoretical and empirical work to understand, predict, and control emergent collective agents in multi-agent AI systems, addressing important safety concerns.

title: Cloud Is Closer Than It Appears: Revisiting the Tradeoffs of Distributed Real-Time Inference
summary: The increasing deployment of deep neural networks (DNNs) in cyber-physical systems (CPS) enhances perception fidelity, but imposes substantial computational demands on execution platforms, posing challenges to real-time control deadlines. Traditional distributed CPS architectures typically favor on-device inference to avoid network variability and contention-induced delays on remote platforms. However, this design choice places significant energy and computational demands on the local hardware. In this work, we revisit the assumption that cloud-based inference is intrinsically unsuitable for latency-sensitive control tasks. We demonstrate that, when provisioned with high-throughput compute resources, cloud platforms can effectively amortize network and queueing delays, enabling them to match or surpass on-device performance for real-time decision-making. Specifically, we develop a formal analytical model that characterizes distributed inference latency as a function of the sensing frequency, platform throughput, network delay, and task-specific safety constraints. We instantiate this model in the context of emergency braking for autonomous driving and validate it through extensive simulations using real-time vehicular dynamics. Our empirical results identify concrete conditions under which cloud-based inference adheres to safety margins more reliably than its on-device counterpart. These findings challenge prevailing design strategies and suggest that the cloud is not merely a feasible option, but often the preferred inference location for distributed CPS architectures. In this light, the cloud is not as distant as traditionally perceived; in fact, it is closer than it appears.

AI.tldr: Cloud-based inference can outperform on-device inference for real-time control tasks in cyber-physical systems when provisioned with high-throughput compute, challenging traditional design assumptions.
AI.motivation: Deep neural networks enhance CPS perception but create computational demands that challenge real-time control. Traditional distributed CPS architectures use on-device inference to avoid network delays, but this places heavy energy and computational demands on local hardware.
AI.method: Developed a formal analytical model characterizing distributed inference latency as function of sensing frequency, platform throughput, network delay, and safety constraints. Validated through extensive simulations using real-time vehicular dynamics in emergency braking context.
AI.result: When provisioned with high-throughput compute, cloud platforms can amortize network and queueing delays to match or surpass on-device performance for real-time decision-making. Empirical results identify conditions where cloud-based inference adheres to safety margins more reliably than on-device.
AI.conclusion: Cloud-based inference is not only feasible but often preferred for distributed CPS architectures, challenging prevailing design strategies. The cloud is closer than traditionally perceived for latency-sensitive control tasks.

title: TADI: Tool-Augmented Drilling Intelligence via Agentic LLM Orchestration over Heterogeneous Wellsite Data
summary: We present TADI (Tool-Augmented Drilling Intelligence), an agentic AI system that transforms drilling operational data into evidence-based analytical intelligence. Applied to the Equinor Volve Field dataset, TADI integrates 1,759 daily drilling reports, selected WITSML real-time objects, 15,634 production records, formation tops, and perforations into a dual-store architecture: DuckDB for structured queries over 12 tables with 65,447 rows, and ChromaDB for semantic search over 36,709 embedded documents. Twelve domain-specialized tools, orchestrated by a large language model via iterative function calling, support multi-step evidence gathering that cross-references structured drilling measurements with daily report narratives. The system parses all 1,759 DDR XML files with zero errors, handles three incompatible well naming conventions, and is backed by 95 automated tests plus a 130-question stress-question taxonomy spanning six operational categories. We formalize the agent's behavior as a sequential tool-selection problem and propose the Evidence Grounding Score (EGS) as a simple grounding-compliance proxy based on measurements, attributed DDR quotations, and required answer sections. The complete 6,084-line, framework-free implementation is reproducible given the public Volve download and an API key, and the case studies and qualitative ablation analysis suggest that domain-specialized tool design, rather than model scale alone, is the primary driver of analytical quality in technical operations.

AI.tldr: TADI is an agentic AI system that transforms drilling data into analytical intelligence using specialized tools, dual database architecture, and LLM orchestration, achieving high accuracy on the Volve Field dataset.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of extracting evidence-based analytical intelligence from complex, heterogeneous drilling operational data (daily reports, real-time measurements, production records) that exists in multiple formats and naming conventions.
AI.method: TADI uses a dual-store architecture: DuckDB for structured queries over 12 tables, and ChromaDB for semantic search over embedded documents. It employs 12 domain-specialized tools orchestrated by an LLM via iterative function calling for multi-step evidence gathering that cross-references structured measurements with daily report narratives.
AI.result: Successfully parsed all 1,759 DDR XML files with zero errors, handled three incompatible well naming conventions, and demonstrated high reliability through 95 automated tests and a 130-question stress taxonomy. The Evidence Grounding Score (EGS) was proposed as a grounding-compliance proxy.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specialized tool design, rather than model scale alone, is the primary driver of analytical quality in technical operations. The complete 6,084-line implementation is reproducible with the public Volve dataset and an API key.

title: High-Probability Convergence in Decentralized Stochastic Optimization with Gradient Tracking
comment: 49 pages, 4 figures. arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2510.06141
summary: We study high-probability (HP) convergence guarantees in decentralized stochastic optimization, where multiple agents collaborate to jointly train a model over a network. Existing HP results in decentralized settings almost exclusively focus on the Decentralized Stochastic Gradient Descent ($\mathtt{DSGD}$) algorithm, which requires strong assumptions, such as bounded data heterogeneity, or strong convexity of each agent's cost. This is contrary to the mean-squared error (MSE) results, where methods incorporating bias-correction techniques are known to converge under relaxed assumptions and achieve better practical performance. In this paper we provide the first step toward bridging the gap, by studying HP convergence of $\mathtt{DSGD}$ incorporating the gradient tracking technique, in the presence of noise satisfying a relaxed sub-Gaussian condition. We show that the resulting method, dubbed $\mathtt{GT-DSGD}$, achieves order-optimal HP convergence rates for both non-convex and Polyak-Łojasiewicz costs, of order $\mathcal{O}\Big(\frac{\log(1/δ)}{\sqrt{nT}}\Big)$ and $\mathcal{O}\Big(\frac{\log(1/δ)}{nT}\Big)$, respectively, where $n$ is the number of agents, $T$ is the time horizon and $δ\in (0,1)$ is the confidence parameter. Our results establish that $\mathtt{GT-DSGD}$ converges in the HP sense under the same conditions on the cost as in the MSE sense, while achieving comparable transient times. To the best of our knowledge, these are the first HP guarantees for decentralized optimization methods incorporating bias-correction. Numerical experiments on real and synthetic data verify our theoretical findings, underlining the superior performance of $\mathtt{GT-DSGD}$ and highlighting that the benefits of incorporating bias-correction are also maintained in the HP sense.

AI.tldr: First high-probability convergence guarantees for decentralized optimization with gradient tracking (GT-DSGD) that work under relaxed assumptions and match mean-squared error performance.
AI.motivation: Existing high-probability (HP) convergence results in decentralized optimization are limited to basic DSGD with strong assumptions (bounded data heterogeneity or strong convexity), while bias-correction methods like gradient tracking are known to perform better in mean-squared error sense but lack HP guarantees.
AI.method: GT-DSGD (Decentralized Stochastic Gradient Descent with gradient tracking technique), analyzed under relaxed sub-Gaussian noise conditions for both non-convex and Polyak-Łojasiewicz cost functions.
AI.result: GT-DSGD achieves order-optimal HP convergence rates: O(log(1/δ)/√(nT)) for non-convex costs and O(log(1/δ)/(nT)) for Polyak-Łojasiewicz costs, where n is number of agents, T is time horizon, δ is confidence parameter. Converges under same conditions as MSE analysis with comparable transient times.
AI.conclusion: First HP guarantees for bias-correction methods in decentralized optimization; GT-DSGD bridges gap between HP and MSE results, maintaining practical benefits of bias-correction in HP sense, validated by experiments on real and synthetic data.

title: FedACT: Concurrent Federated Intelligence across Heterogeneous Data Sources
summary: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative intelligence across decentralized data source devices in a privacy-preserving way. While substantial research attention has been drawn to optimizing the learning process for an individual task, real-world applications increasingly require multiple machine learning tasks simultaneously training their models across a shared pool of devices. Naively applying single-FL optimization techniques in multi-FL systems results in suboptimal system performance, particularly due to device heterogeneity and resource inefficiency. To address such a critical open challenge, we introduce {\em FedACT}, a novel resource heterogeneity-aware device scheduling approach designed to efficiently schedule heterogeneous devices across multiple concurrent FL jobs, with the goal of minimizing their average job completion time (JCT). {\em FedACT} dynamically assigns devices to FL jobs based on an alignment scoring mechanism that evaluates the compatibility between available resources of devices and resource demands of jobs. Additionally, it incorporates participation fairness to ensure balanced contributions from devices across jobs, further enhancing the accuracy levels of learned global models. An optimal scheduling plan is formulated in {\em FedACT} by prioritizing devices with higher alignment scores, while ensuring fair participation across jobs. To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed scheduling algorithm, we carried out comprehensive experiments using diverse FL jobs and benchmark datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that {\em FedACT} reduces the average JCT by up to 8.3\(\times\) and improves model accuracy by up to 44.5\%, compared to the state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: FedACT is a resource heterogeneity-aware device scheduling approach for multiple concurrent federated learning jobs that minimizes average job completion time while improving model accuracy through fair device participation.
AI.motivation: Real-world applications increasingly require multiple ML tasks to train simultaneously across shared devices. Single-FL optimization techniques perform poorly in multi-FL systems due to device heterogeneity and resource inefficiency, leading to suboptimal performance.
AI.method: FedACT uses a resource heterogeneity-aware device scheduling approach with an alignment scoring mechanism that evaluates compatibility between device resources and job demands. It incorporates participation fairness to ensure balanced device contributions across jobs, prioritizing devices with higher alignment scores while maintaining fair participation.
AI.result: Experimental results show FedACT reduces average job completion time by up to 8.3× and improves model accuracy by up to 44.5% compared to state-of-the-art baselines, using diverse FL jobs and benchmark datasets.
AI.conclusion: FedACT effectively addresses the critical challenge of scheduling heterogeneous devices across multiple concurrent FL jobs by optimizing both job completion time and model accuracy through intelligent resource-aware scheduling with fairness constraints.

title: AgentReputation: A Decentralized Agentic AI Reputation Framework
comment: 5 pages, 1 figure, accepted to FSE 2026, Ideas, Visions and Reflections track
summary: Decentralized, agentic AI marketplaces are rapidly emerging to support software engineering tasks such as debugging, patch generation, and security auditing, often operating without centralized oversight. However, existing reputation mechanisms fail in this setting for three fundamental reasons: agents can strategically optimize against evaluation procedures; demonstrated competence does not reliably transfer across heterogeneous task contexts; and verification rigor varies widely, from lightweight automated checks to costly expert review. Current approaches to reputation drawing on federated learning, blockchain-based AI platforms, and large language model safety research are unable to address these challenges in combination. We therefore propose \textbf{AgentReputation}, a decentralized, three-layer reputation framework for agentic AI systems. The framework separates task execution, reputation services, and tamper-proof persistence to both leverage their respective strengths and enable independent evolution. The framework introduces explicit verification regimes linked to agent reputation metadata, as well as context-conditioned reputation cards that prevent reputation conflation across domains and task types. In addition, AgentReputation provides a decision-facing policy engine that supports resource allocation, access control, and adaptive verification escalation based on risk and uncertainty. Building on this framework, we outline several future research directions, including the development of verification ontologies, methods for quantifying verification strength, privacy-preserving evidence mechanisms, cold-start reputation bootstrapping, and defenses against adversarial manipulation.

AI.tldr: AgentReputation is a decentralized three-layer reputation framework for AI agent marketplaces that addresses strategic gaming, context transfer issues, and verification variability through explicit verification regimes, context-conditioned reputation cards, and decision-facing policy engines.
AI.motivation: Existing reputation mechanisms fail in decentralized AI agent marketplaces because agents can strategically optimize against evaluations, competence doesn't reliably transfer across heterogeneous tasks, and verification rigor varies widely from automated checks to expert review. Current approaches from federated learning, blockchain-based AI platforms, and LLM safety research can't address these challenges in combination.
AI.method: Proposes AgentReputation, a decentralized three-layer framework separating task execution, reputation services, and tamper-proof persistence. Introduces explicit verification regimes linked to reputation metadata, context-conditioned reputation cards to prevent cross-domain conflation, and a decision-facing policy engine for resource allocation and adaptive verification escalation.
AI.result: A comprehensive framework that enables independent evolution of different components while addressing key challenges: preventing strategic gaming through proper separation, handling context heterogeneity via reputation cards, and managing verification variability through explicit regimes and adaptive escalation.
AI.conclusion: AgentReputation provides a foundational architecture for trustworthy decentralized AI marketplaces and outlines future research directions including verification ontologies, verification strength quantification, privacy-preserving evidence, cold-start bootstrapping, and adversarial manipulation defenses.

title: Meritocratic Fairness in Budgeted Combinatorial Multi-armed Bandits via Shapley Values
summary: We propose a new framework for meritocratic fairness in budgeted combinatorial multi-armed bandits with full-bandit feedback (BCMAB-FBF). Unlike semi-bandit feedback, the contribution of individual arms is not received in full-bandit feedback, making the setting significantly more challenging. To compute arm contributions in BCMAB-FBF, we first extend the Shapley value, a classical solution concept from cooperative game theory, to the $K$-Shapley value, which captures the marginal contribution of an agent restricted to a set of size at most $K$. We show that $K$-Shapley value is a unique solution concept that satisfies Symmetry, Linearity, Null player, and efficiency properties. We next propose K-SVFair-FBF, a fairness-aware bandit algorithm that adaptively estimates $K$-Shapley value with unknown valuation function. Unlike standard bandit literature on full bandit feedback, K-SVFair-FBF not only learns the valuation function under full feedback setting but also mitigates the noise arising from Monte Carlo approximations. Theoretically, we prove that K-SVFair-FBF achieves $O(T^{3/4})$ regret bound on fairness regret. Through experiments on federated learning and social influence maximization datasets, we demonstrate that our approach achieves fairness and performs more effectively than existing baselines.

AI.tldr: A novel framework for meritocratic fairness in combinatorial bandits with full-bandit feedback using K-Shapley values to measure arm contributions and a fairness-aware algorithm achieving O(T^{3/4}) regret.
AI.motivation: Addressing the challenge of meritocratic fairness in budgeted combinatorial multi-armed bandits with full-bandit feedback, where individual arm contributions are not observable, unlike semi-bandit feedback settings.
AI.method: Proposes K-SVFair-FBF: 1) Extends Shapley value to K-Shapley value capturing marginal contributions within sets of size K; 2) Develops fairness-aware bandit algorithm estimating K-Shapley values adaptively with unknown valuation function; 3) Mitigates noise from Monte Carlo approximations in full feedback setting.
AI.result: K-SVFair-FBF achieves O(T^{3/4}) regret bound on fairness regret. Experimental results on federated learning and social influence maximization datasets show superior performance in achieving fairness compared to existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses meritocratic fairness in challenging full-bandit feedback settings by leveraging game-theoretic concepts and provides theoretical guarantees with practical effectiveness on real-world applications.

title: What Physics do Data-Driven MoCap-to-Radar Models Learn?
summary: Data-driven MoCap-to-radar models generate plausible micro-Doppler spectrograms, but do they actually learn the underlying physics? We introduce a physics-based interpretability framework to answer this question via two proposed complementary metrics: one measures alignment between model predictions and the physics-derived Doppler frequency, while the other tests whether predictions preserve the velocity-frequency relationship under velocity intervention. Both metrics require only MoCap input and model predictions, without access to measured radar data. Experiments across several model architectures reveal that low reconstruction error does not guarantee physical consistency: some, but not all, models achieve low error yet perform poorly on the two physics-based metrics. Further analysis shows that temporal attention is critical for transformer-based models to learn the underlying physics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a physics-based interpretability framework to evaluate whether neural networks learning MoCap-to-radar translation actually capture physical dynamics, showing that low reconstruction error doesn't guarantee physical consistency.
AI.motivation: Current data-driven MoCap-to-radar models generate plausible spectrograms, but it's unclear whether they actually learn the underlying physics of micro-Doppler signatures rather than just learning statistical patterns.
AI.method: Proposed two complementary physics-based metrics: 1) measures alignment between model predictions and physics-derived Doppler frequency, 2) tests whether predictions preserve velocity-frequency relationship under velocity intervention. The framework only requires MoCap input and model predictions (no measured radar data).
AI.result: Experiments show low reconstruction error doesn't guarantee physical consistency - some models achieve low error but perform poorly on physics-based metrics. Temporal attention is identified as critical for transformer-based models to learn underlying physics.
AI.conclusion: Need to go beyond reconstruction error to assess whether neural networks truly learn physical dynamics; the proposed physics-based interpretability framework provides necessary tools, with temporal attention emerging as key for physics learning.

title: Minimal, Local, Causal Explanations for Jailbreak Success in Large Language Models
summary: Safety trained large language models (LLMs) can often be induced to answer harmful requests through jailbreak prompts. Because we lack a robust understanding of why LLMs are susceptible to jailbreaks, future frontier models operating more autonomously in higher-stakes settings may similarly be vulnerable to such attacks. Prior work has studied jailbreak success by examining the model's intermediate representations, identifying directions in this space that causally encode concepts like harmfulness and refusal. Then, they globally explain all jailbreak attacks as attempting to reduce or strengthen these concepts (e.g., reduce harmfulness). However, different jailbreak strategies may succeed by strengthening or suppressing different intermediate concepts, and the same jailbreak strategy may not work for different harmful request categories (e.g., violence vs. cyberattack); thus, we seek to give a local explanation -- i.e., why did this specific jailbreak succeed? To address this gap, we introduce LOCA, a method that gives Local, CAusal explanations of jailbreak success by identifying a minimal set of interpretable, intermediate representation changes that causally induce model refusal on an otherwise successful jailbreak request. We evaluate LOCA on harmful original-jailbreak pairs from a large jailbreak benchmark across Gemma and Llama chat models, comparing against prior methods adapted to this setting. LOCA can successfully induce refusal by making, on average, six interpretable changes; prior work routinely fails to achieve refusal even after 20 changes. LOCA is a step toward mechanistic, local explanations of jailbreak success in LLMs. Code to be released.

AI.tldr: LOCA is a method that provides local, causal explanations for jailbreak success in LLMs by identifying minimal sets of interpretable intermediate representation changes that induce model refusal.
AI.motivation: Current approaches provide global explanations for jailbreak attacks by examining broad concepts like harmfulness, but different jailbreak strategies may succeed by targeting different intermediate concepts. The same jailbreak may not work across different harmful request categories, necessitating local explanations for specific jailbreak success cases.
AI.method: LOCA (Local, CAusal explanations) identifies minimal sets of interpretable changes to intermediate representations that causally induce model refusal on otherwise successful jailbreak requests. It focuses on specific jailbreak strategies rather than broad global explanations.
AI.result: LOCA successfully induces refusal with an average of six interpretable changes, while prior methods fail even after 20 changes. The method was evaluated on harmful original-jailbreak pairs from a large jailbreak benchmark across Gemma and Llama chat models.
AI.conclusion: LOCA represents progress toward mechanistic, local explanations of jailbreak success in LLMs, addressing the gap in understanding why specific jailbreaks succeed by providing interpretable causal explanations rather than broad global ones.

title: RunAgent: Interpreting Natural-Language Plans with Constraint-Guided Execution
summary: Humans solve problems by executing targeted plans, yet large language models (LLMs) remain unreliable for structured workflow execution. We propose RunAgent, a multi-agent plan execution platform that interprets natural-language plans while enforcing stepwise execution through constraints and rubrics. RunAgent bridges the expressiveness of natural language with the determinism of programming via an agentic language with explicit control constructs (e.g., \texttt{IF}, \texttt{GOTO}, \texttt{FORALL}). Beyond verifying syntactic and semantic verification of the step output, which is performed based on the specific instruction of each step, RunAgent autonomously derives and validates constraints based on the description of the task and its instance at each step. RunAgent also dynamically selects among LLM-based reasoning, tool usage, and code generation and execution (e.g., in Python), and incorporates error correction mechanisms to ensure correctness. Finally, RunAgent filters the context history by retaining only relevant information during the execution of each step. Evaluations on Natural-plan and SciBench Datasets demonstrate that RunAgent outperforms baseline LLMs and state-of-the-art PlanGEN methods.

AI.tldr: RunAgent is a multi-agent platform that executes natural-language plans using explicit control constructs, dynamically selects execution methods, and includes error correction, outperforming existing methods on benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: Despite humans' ability to solve problems through targeted plans, large language models remain unreliable for structured workflow execution. There's a need to bridge the gap between natural language expressiveness and deterministic program execution for complex task automation.
AI.method: RunAgent uses a multi-agent system that interprets natural-language plans with explicit control constructs (IF, GOTO, FORALL). It performs stepwise execution with constraints and rubrics, dynamically selecting among LLM-based reasoning, tool usage, and code generation/execution. The system includes semantic verification, autonomous constraint derivation, error correction mechanisms, and dynamic context filtering.
AI.result: RunAgent outperforms baseline LLMs and state-of-the-art PlanGEN methods on Natural-plan and SciBench Datasets, demonstrating superior structured workflow execution capabilities.
AI.conclusion: RunAgent successfully bridges natural language expressiveness with deterministic programming through its agentic language and multi-agent architecture, providing reliable structured workflow execution while maintaining the flexibility of natural language planning.

title: AirFM-DDA: Air-Interface Foundation Model in the Delay-Doppler-Angle Domain for AI-Native 6G
summary: The success of large foundation models is catalyzing a new paradigm for AI-native 6G network design: wireless foundation models for physical layer design. However, existing models often operate on channel state information (CSI) in the space-time-frequency (STF) domain, where distinct multipath components are inherently superimposed and structurally entangled. This hinders the learning of universal channel representation. Meanwhile, their reliance on global attention mechanisms incurs prohibitive computational overhead. In this paper, we propose AirFM-DDA, an Air-interface Foundation Model operating in the Delay-Doppler-Angle (DDA) domain for physicallayer tasks. Specifically, AirFM-DDA reparameterizes CSI from the STF domain into the DDA domain to explicitly resolve multipath components along physically meaningful axes. It employs a window-based attention module augmented with framestructure-aware positional encoding (FS-PE). This window-based attention aligns with locally clustered multipath dependencies while avoiding quadratic-complexity global attention, and FS-PE injects frame-structure priors into network. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AirFM-DDA achieves superior zero-shot generalization across unseen scenarios and datasets, consistently outperforming the baselines on channel prediction and estimation tasks. Compared to the global attention, its window-based attention reduces training and inference costs by nearly an order of magnitude. Moreover, AirFM-DDA maintains robustness under high mobility, large delay spreads, severe noise, and extreme aliasing conditions.

AI.tldr: AirFM-DDA is a wireless foundation model for 6G networks that operates in the Delay-Doppler-Angle domain instead of space-time-frequency domain to disentangle multipath components and uses efficient window-based attention with frame-structure positional encoding for physical layer tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing foundation models for 6G wireless networks face two key problems: they operate in space-time-frequency domain where multipath components are superimposed and entangled, hindering universal channel representation learning; and they use computationally expensive global attention mechanisms.
AI.method: AirFM-DDA reparameterizes channel state information from space-time-frequency domain to delay-Doppler-angle domain to explicitly resolve multipath components along physically meaningful axes. It employs window-based attention (instead of quadratic-complexity global attention) aligned with locally clustered multipath dependencies, augmented with frame-structure-aware positional encoding to inject priors.
AI.result: AirFM-DDA achieves superior zero-shot generalization across unseen scenarios and datasets, outperforming baselines on channel prediction and estimation tasks. Its window-based attention reduces training/inference costs by nearly an order of magnitude compared to global attention. The model maintains robustness under challenging conditions including high mobility, large delay spreads, severe noise, and extreme aliasing.
AI.conclusion: Operating in delay-Doppler-angle domain with efficient window-based attention enables AirFM-DDA to learn universal channel representations with dramatically reduced computational cost while achieving strong generalization and robustness for physical layer tasks in AI-native 6G networks.

title: Are Tools All We Need? Unveiling the Tool-Use Tax in LLM Agents
summary: Tool-augmented reasoning has become a popular direction for LLM-based agents, and it is widely assumed to improve reasoning and reliability. However, we demonstrate that this consensus does not always hold: in the presence of semantic distractors, tool-augmented reasoning does not necessarily outperform native CoT. To explain this performance gap, we propose a Factorized Intervention Framework that isolates the cost of prompt formatting, the overhead of the tool-calling protocol, and the actual gain from executing tools. Our analysis reveals a critical tradeoff: under semantic noise, the gains from tools often fail to offset the "tool-use tax", which is the performance degradation introduced by the tool-calling protocol itself. To address this, we introduce G-STEP, a lightweight inference-time gate to mitigate protocol-induced errors. While this yields partial recovery, our findings suggest that more substantial improvements still require strengthening the model's intrinsic reasoning and tool-interaction capabilities.

AI.tldr: Tool-augmented reasoning doesn't always outperform standard Chain-of-Thought under semantic distractors due to a "tool-use tax" that outweighs benefits, but a lightweight gate (G-STEP) can partially mitigate this issue.
AI.motivation: The paper challenges the common assumption that tool-augmented reasoning always improves LLM performance and reliability. It identifies a gap where semantic distractors cause tool-augmented reasoning to underperform compared to native CoT.
AI.method: Proposes a Factorized Intervention Framework to isolate three components: prompt formatting cost, tool-calling protocol overhead, and actual tool execution gains. Then introduces G-STEP, a lightweight inference-time gate to mitigate protocol-induced errors.
AI.result: Analysis reveals a critical tradeoff: under semantic noise, tool gains often fail to offset the "tool-use tax" - performance degradation from the tool-calling protocol. G-STEP yields partial recovery but substantial improvements still require stronger intrinsic reasoning and tool-interaction capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Tool-augmented reasoning has limitations under semantic distractors, and while partial solutions exist, fundamental improvements require enhancing models' intrinsic reasoning and tool-interaction abilities rather than just protocol optimization.

title: Learning physically grounded traffic accident reconstruction from public accident reports
summary: Traffic accidents are routinely documented in textual reports, yet physically grounded accident reconstruction remains difficult because detailed scene measurements and expert reconstructions are scarce, costly and hard to scale. Here we formulate accident reconstruction from publicly accessible reports and scene measurements as a parameterized multimodal learning problem. We construct CISS-REC, a dataset of 6,217 real-world accident cases curated from the NHTSA Crash Investigation Sampling System, and develop a reconstruction framework that grounds report semantics to road topology and participant attributes, reconstructs lane consistent pre-impact motion, and refines collision relevant interactions through localized geometric reasoning and temporal allocation. Our method outperforms representative baselines on CISS-REC, achieving the strongest overall reconstruction fidelity, including improved accident point accuracy and collision consistency. These results show that public accident reports can serve as scalable computational substrates for quantitatively verifiable accident reconstruction, with potential value for traffic safety analysis, simulation and autonomous driving research.

AI.tldr: A novel framework that reconstructs traffic accidents from textual reports using multimodal learning, outperforming baselines on a new dataset of real-world crashes.
AI.motivation: Traffic accident reconstruction is currently difficult, expensive, and hard to scale because it requires detailed scene measurements and expert analysis. Publicly accessible reports exist but lack the physical grounding needed for reconstruction.
AI.method: Formulates accident reconstruction as a parameterized multimodal learning problem. Creates CISS-REC dataset (6,217 real-world cases from NHTSA). Develops a framework that: (1) grounds report semantics to road topology and participant attributes, (2) reconstructs lane-consistent pre-impact motion, and (3) refines collision interactions through localized geometric reasoning and temporal allocation.
AI.result: Outperforms representative baselines on CISS-REC dataset, achieving strongest overall reconstruction fidelity with improved accident point accuracy and collision consistency.
AI.conclusion: Public accident reports can serve as scalable computational substrates for quantitatively verifiable accident reconstruction, with valuable applications for traffic safety analysis, simulation, and autonomous driving research.

title: TUR-DPO: Topology- and Uncertainty-Aware Direct Preference Optimization
comment: Proceedings of the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences is commonly done via reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) or, more simply, via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). While DPO is stable and RL-free, it treats preferences as flat winner vs. loser signals and is sensitive to noisy or brittle preferences arising from fragile chains of thought. We propose TUR-DPO, a topology- and uncertainty-aware variant of DPO that rewards how answers are derived, not only what they say, by eliciting lightweight reasoning topologies and combining semantic faithfulness, utility, and topology quality into a calibrated uncertainty signal. A small learnable reward is factorized over these signals and incorporated into an uncertainty-weighted DPO objective that remains RL-free and relies only on a fixed or moving reference policy. Empirically, across open 7-8B models and benchmarks spanning mathematical reasoning, factual question answering, summarization, and helpful/harmless dialogue, TUR-DPO improves judge win-rates, faithfulness, and calibration relative to DPO while preserving training simplicity and avoiding online rollouts. We further observe consistent gains in multimodal and long-context settings, and show that TUR-DPO matches or exceeds PPO on reasoning-centric tasks while maintaining operational simplicity.

AI.tldr: TUR-DPO improves DPO by incorporating reasoning topology and uncertainty signals to better align LLMs with human preferences, achieving better performance while maintaining training simplicity.
AI.motivation: DPO is stable and RL-free but treats preferences as simple winner/loser signals, making it sensitive to noisy or brittle preferences arising from fragile reasoning chains. Current approaches fail to account for how answers are derived and lack proper uncertainty handling.
AI.method: TUR-DPO introduces a topology- and uncertainty-aware variant of DPO that rewards answer derivation processes by eliciting lightweight reasoning topologies and combining semantic faithfulness, utility, and topology quality into calibrated uncertainty signals. Uses a small learnable reward factorized over these signals with an uncertainty-weighted DPO objective that remains RL-free.
AI.result: Across 7-8B models and benchmarks spanning mathematical reasoning, factual QA, summarization, and dialogue, TUR-DPO improves judge win-rates, faithfulness, and calibration relative to DPO. Shows consistent gains in multimodal and long-context settings, matching or exceeding PPO on reasoning tasks while maintaining operational simplicity.
AI.conclusion: TUR-DPO successfully addresses DPO's limitations by incorporating reasoning process evaluation and uncertainty calibration, achieving better alignment performance without sacrificing training simplicity or requiring online rollouts.

title: Smart Ensemble Learning Framework for Predicting Groundwater Heavy Metal Pollution
comment: 53 pages, 16 figures, accepted for publication in Earth Systems and Environment (2026)
summary: Groundwater in the Densu Basin is increasingly threatened by heavy metal contamination, but conventional methods fail to capture the statistical complexity and spatial heterogeneity of pollution indicators. A key challenge is modelling the Heavy Metal Pollution Index (HPI), which is typically skewed and affected by correlated contaminants, leading to biased predictions without transformation. This study develops a predictive framework integrating response transformations with nested cross-validated ensemble machine learning. Three transformations (raw, log, and Gaussian copula) were applied to HPI and evaluated across six learners: support vector regression (SVM), $k$-nearest neighbours (k-NN), CART, Elastic Net, kernel ridge regression, and a stacked Lasso ensemble. Raw-scale models produced deceptively high fits (Elastic Net and stacked ensemble $R^2 \approx 1.0$), suggesting over-optimism. The log transformation stabilised variance (SVM: $R^2 = 0.93$, RMSE $= 0.18$; k-NN: $R^2 = 0.92$, RMSE $= 0.20$). The Gaussian copula gave the most reliable results: stacked ensemble $R^2 = 0.96$ (RMSE $= 0.19$), with other learners maintaining high accuracy. Copula-based models improved residuals and produced spatially plausible maps. DBSCAN clustering revealed Fe and Mn as primary HPI contributors, consistent with regional hydrogeochemistry. Limitations include reliance on random (not spatial) cross-validation and basin-specific scope. Future work should explore spatial validation and other geological settings. Overall, distribution-aware ensembles with clustering diagnostics offer robust, interpretable assessments of groundwater contamination.

AI.tldr: A machine learning framework using response transformations and nested cross-validation to predict groundwater Heavy Metal Pollution Index (HPI), with Gaussian copula transformation yielding the most reliable results and spatial clustering identifying key contaminant contributors.
AI.motivation: Groundwater in the Densu Basin faces increasing heavy metal contamination, but conventional methods fail to capture the statistical complexity and spatial heterogeneity of pollution indicators. Traditional approaches struggle with skewed HPI distributions and correlated contaminants, leading to biased predictions without proper transformation.
AI.method: Developed a predictive framework integrating response transformations (raw, log, and Gaussian copula) with nested cross-validated ensemble machine learning. Evaluated six learners: SVM, k-NN, CART, Elastic Net, kernel ridge regression, and a stacked Lasso ensemble. Applied DBSCAN clustering to identify key HPI contributors.
AI.result: Raw-scale models showed deceptive over-optimism (R²≈1.0). Log transformation stabilized variance (SVM: R²=0.93, RMSE=0.18; k-NN: R²=0.92, RMSE=0.20). Gaussian copula transformation yielded most reliable results: stacked ensemble R²=0.96, RMSE=0.19, with improved residuals and spatially plausible maps. DBSCAN identified Fe and Mn as primary HPI contributors.
AI.conclusion: Distribution-aware ensembles with clustering diagnostics offer robust, interpretable assessments of groundwater contamination. Future work should explore spatial validation and other geological settings to improve generalizability beyond the Densu Basin-specific scope.

title: ARMOR 2025: A Military-Aligned Benchmark for Evaluating Large Language Model Safety Beyond Civilian Contexts
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are now being explored for defense applications that require reliable and legally compliant decision support. They also hold significant potential to enhance decision making, coordination, and operational efficiency in military contexts. These uses demand evaluation methods that reflect the doctrinal standards that guide real military operations. Existing safety benchmarks focus on general social risks and do not test whether models follow the legal and ethical rules that govern real military operations. To address this gap, we introduce ARMOR 2025, a military aligned safety benchmark grounded in three core military doctrines the Law of War, the Rules of Engagement, and the Joint Ethics Regulation. We extract doctrinal text from these sources and generate multiple choice questions that preserve the intended meaning of each rule. The benchmark is organized through a taxonomy informed by the Observe Orient Decide Act (OODA) decision making framework. This structure enables systematic testing of accuracy and refusal across military relevant decision types. This benchmark features a structured 12-category taxonomy, 519 doctrinally grounded prompts, and rigorous evaluation procedures applied to 21 commercial LLMs. Evaluation results reveal critical gaps in safety alignment for military applications.

AI.tldr: ARMOR 2025 is a military safety benchmark that evaluates LLMs on three core military doctrines (Law of War, Rules of Engagement, Joint Ethics Regulation) using the OODA framework to test compliance with real-world military operational standards.
AI.motivation: LLMs are being explored for military decision support applications, but existing safety benchmarks focus on general social risks and fail to test whether models follow the specific legal, ethical, and doctrinal rules that govern real military operations.
AI.method: The researchers created ARMOR 2025 by extracting doctrinal text from three military sources (Law of War, Rules of Engagement, Joint Ethics Regulation), generating multiple choice questions that preserve rule meanings, and organizing them through an OODA-based taxonomy. They developed 519 doctrinally grounded prompts with rigorous evaluation procedures.
AI.result: Evaluation of 21 commercial LLMs revealed critical gaps in safety alignment for military applications, showing that current models don't adequately follow military doctrines and legal rules required for reliable defense applications.
AI.conclusion: The paper introduces a necessary military-aligned safety benchmark that addresses the gap between general LLM safety evaluation and the specific doctrinal requirements of military applications, revealing significant deficiencies that must be addressed for reliable military use of LLMs.

title: Information-Theoretic Generalization Bounds for Stochastic Gradient Descent with Predictable Virtual Noise
summary:
Information-theoretic generalization bounds analyze stochastic optimization by relating expected generalization error to the mutual information between learned parameters and training data. Virtual perturbation analyses of SGD add auxiliary Gaussian noise only in the proof, making mutual information tractable while leaving the actual SGD trajectory unchanged. Existing bounds, however, typically require perturbation covariances to be fixed independently of the optimization history, limiting their ability to represent geometries induced by moving gradient statistics, preconditioners, curvature proxies, and other pathwise information.
  We introduce predictable history-adaptive virtual perturbations, where the perturbation covariance at each iteration may depend on the past real SGD history but not on current or future randomness. This predictability enables a conditional Gaussian relative-entropy argument and yields generalization bounds for SGD with adaptive virtual-noise geometry. The bounds replace fixed sensitivity and gradient-deviation terms with conditional adaptive counterparts, include an output-sensitivity penalty from accumulated perturbation covariance, and reduce the deviation term to a conditional variance only under conditional unbiasedness.
  Since adaptive covariances may be data-dependent, we separate local Gaussian smoothing from global reference-kernel comparison. The resulting bound includes a covariance-comparison cost measuring the KL price of using an admissible reference geometry different from the actual adaptive covariance. Fixed-noise-style bounds are recovered under admissible synchronization, such as deterministic, public, or prefix-observable covariance rules. The framework recovers fixed isotropic and geometry-aware bounds as special cases while extending virtual perturbation analysis to history-dependent SGD without modifying the algorithm.

AI.tldr: Introduces history-adaptive virtual perturbations for SGD generalization bounds, allowing perturbation covariances to depend on past optimization history while maintaining tractability through conditional Gaussian analysis, extending previous fixed-noise bounds to adaptive geometries.
AI.motivation: Existing information-theoretic generalization bounds for SGD use fixed virtual perturbation covariances independent of optimization history, which limits their ability to capture important geometric information from moving gradient statistics, preconditioners, curvature proxies, and other pathwise information that arises during optimization.
AI.method: Proposes predictable history-adaptive virtual perturbations where perturbation covariance at each iteration can depend on past real SGD history (but not current/future randomness). Uses conditional Gaussian relative-entropy argument to derive generalization bounds, separating local Gaussian smoothing from global reference-kernel comparison, with a covariance-comparison cost for using admissible reference geometries.
AI.result: Derives generalization bounds for SGD with adaptive virtual-noise geometry that replace fixed sensitivity and gradient-deviation terms with conditional adaptive counterparts, include output-sensitivity penalty from accumulated perturbation covariance, and reduce deviation term to conditional variance under conditional unbiasedness. The framework recovers fixed-noise bounds under admissible synchronization conditions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework extends virtual perturbation analysis to history-dependent SGD without algorithm modification, capturing adaptive optimization geometries while maintaining tractability, and generalizes previous fixed isotropic and geometry-aware bounds as special cases.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Meta Bayesian Optimization for Fusion Energy and Scientific Applications
comment: Accepted at IJCAI 2026 (35th International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence)
summary: Inertial Confinement Fusion (ICF) holds transformative promise for sustainable, near-limitless clean energy, yet remains constrained by prohibitively high costs and limited experimental opportunities. This paper presents Human-in-the-Loop Meta Bayesian Optimization (HL-MBO), a framework that integrates expert knowledge with few-shot, uncertainty-aware machine learning to accelerate discovery in data-scarce, high-stakes scientific domains. HL-MBO introduces a meta-learned surrogate model with an expert-informed acquisition function to recommend candidate experiments. To foster trust and enable informed decisions, HL-MBO also provides interpretable explanations of its suggestions. We show HL-MBO outperforms current BO methods on ICF energy yield optimization, as well as benchmarks in molecular optimization and critical temperature maximization for superconducting materials.

AI.tldr: HL-MBO framework combines expert knowledge with meta Bayesian optimization to accelerate discovery in data-scarce scientific domains like inertial confinement fusion, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Inertial Confinement Fusion (ICF) has huge potential for clean energy but faces prohibitive costs and limited experimental opportunities, requiring more efficient discovery methods in data-scarce, high-stakes scientific domains.
AI.method: Human-in-the-Loop Meta Bayesian Optimization (HL-MBO) integrates expert knowledge with few-shot, uncertainty-aware machine learning using a meta-learned surrogate model and expert-informed acquisition function, plus interpretable explanations for trust building.
AI.result: HL-MBO outperforms current Bayesian optimization methods on ICF energy yield optimization, and also shows superior performance on molecular optimization and critical temperature maximization for superconducting materials benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The HL-MBO framework successfully accelerates discovery in data-scarce, high-stakes scientific domains by effectively combining human expertise with machine learning, offering a promising approach for complex optimization problems like ICF energy yield.

title: Agentic AI for Trip Planning Optimization Application
summary: Trip planning for intelligent vehicles increasingly requires selecting optimal routes rather than merely producing feasible itineraries, as interacting factors such as travel time, energy consumption, and traffic conditions directly affect plan quality. Yet existing systems are largely designed for feasibility-oriented planning, and current benchmarks provide only reference answers without ground truth, preventing objective evaluation of optimization performance. In our paper, we address these limitations with an agentic AI framework that enables dynamic refinement through an orchestration agent coordinating specialized agents for traffic, charging, and points of interest, and with the Trip-planning Optimization Problems Dataset, which supplies definitive optimal solutions and category-level task structure for fine-grained analysis. Experiments show that our system achieves 77.4\% accuracy on the TOP Benchmark, significantly outperforming single-agent and workflow-based multi-agent baselines, demonstrating the importance of orchestrated agentic reasoning for robust trip planning optimization.

AI.tldr: Agentic AI framework with orchestration agent coordinates specialized agents for traffic, charging, and POIs to optimize trip planning, achieving 77.4% accuracy on new TOP Benchmark with ground truth solutions.
AI.motivation: Existing trip planning systems focus on feasibility rather than optimization, and current benchmarks lack ground truth solutions, preventing objective evaluation of optimization performance.
AI.method: Proposes an agentic AI framework with an orchestration agent that dynamically coordinates specialized agents (traffic, charging, points of interest) for trip optimization, along with the Trip-planning Optimization Problems Dataset providing definitive optimal solutions for evaluation.
AI.result: The system achieves 77.4% accuracy on the TOP Benchmark, significantly outperforming both single-agent and workflow-based multi-agent baselines.
AI.conclusion: Orchestrated agentic reasoning is crucial for robust trip planning optimization, as demonstrated by the superior performance of the proposed framework over existing approaches.

title: Soft-MSM: Differentiable Context-Aware Elastic Alignment for Time Series
summary:
Elastic distances like dynamic time warping (DTW) are central to time series machine learning because they compare sequences under local temporal misalignment. Soft-DTW is an adaptation of DTW that can be used as a gradient-based loss by replacing the hard minimum in its dynamic-programming recursion with a smooth relaxation. However, this approach does not directly extend to elastic distances whose transition costs depend on the local alignment context. Move-Split-Merge (MSM) is one such distance: it uses context-aware split and merge penalties and has often outperformed DTW in supervised and unsupervised time series machine learning tasks such as classification and clustering.
  We introduce Soft-MSM, a smooth relaxation of MSM and an elastic alignment loss with context-aware transition costs. Central to the formulation is a smooth gated surrogate for MSM's piecewise split/merge cost, which enables gradients through both the dynamic-programming recursion and the local transition structure. We derive the forward recursion, backward recursion, soft alignment matrix, closed-form gradient, limiting behaviour, and divergence-corrected formulation. Experiments on 112 UCR datasets show that Soft-MSM gives lower MSM barycentre loss than existing MSM barycentre methods, and yields significantly better clustering and nearest-centroid classification performance than Soft-DTW-based alternatives. An implementation is available in the open-source \texttt{aeon} toolkit.

AI.tldr: Soft-MSM introduces a smooth relaxation of the Move-Split-Merge (MSM) distance for time series analysis, enabling gradient-based optimization with context-aware transition costs, outperforming Soft-DTW and existing MSM methods in clustering and classification tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing smooth elastic distances like Soft-DTW don't extend well to distances with context-aware transition costs like MSM. MSM has shown superior performance to DTW in various time series tasks but lacks a smooth, gradient-friendly formulation needed for modern machine learning optimization.
AI.method: Developed Soft-MSM by creating a smooth gated surrogate for MSM's piecewise split/merge costs, enabling gradients through both the dynamic-programming recursion and local transition structure. Derived forward/backward recursions, soft alignment matrix, closed-form gradient, limiting behavior, and divergence-corrected formulation.
AI.result: On 112 UCR datasets, Soft-MSM achieves lower MSM barycentre loss than existing MSM barycentre methods. It yields significantly better clustering and nearest-centroid classification performance compared to Soft-DTW-based alternatives. Implementation available in the open-source aeon toolkit.
AI.conclusion: Soft-MSM successfully extends smooth elastic distance concepts to context-aware distances like MSM, providing an effective gradient-based loss function that outperforms existing methods in practical time series machine learning tasks.

title: Token Arena: A Continuous Benchmark Unifying Energy and Cognition in AI Inference
summary: Public inference benchmarks compare AI systems at the model and provider level, but the unit at which deployment decisions are actually made is the endpoint: the (provider, model, stock-keeping-unit) tuple at which a specific quantization, decoding strategy, region, and serving stack is exposed. We introduce TokenArena, a continuous benchmark that measures inference at endpoint granularity along five core axes (output speed, time to first token, workload-blended price, effective context, and quality on the live endpoint) and synthesizes them, together with a modeled energy estimate, into three headline composites: joules per correct answer, dollars per correct answer, and endpoint fidelity (output-distribution similarity to a first-party reference). The framework's novelty is empirical and methodological. Across 78 endpoints serving 12 model families, the same model on different endpoints differs in mean accuracy by up to 12.5 points on math and code, in fingerprint similarity to first party by up to 12 points, in tail latency by an order of magnitude, and in modeled joules per correct answer by a factor of 6.2. We further show that workload-aware blended pricing reorders the leaderboard substantially: 7 of 10 top-ranked endpoints under the chat preset (3:1 input:output) fall out of the top 10 under the retrieval-augmented preset (20:1), and the reasoning preset (1:5) elevates frontier closed models that the chat preset penalizes on price. We release the framework, schema, probe and eval harness, and a v1.0 leaderboard snapshot under CC BY 4.0. TokenArena is a methodology, not a single ranking; we publish full provenance and limitations and welcome external replication.

AI.tldr: TokenArena is a continuous benchmark for AI inference endpoints that measures performance across five axes (speed, latency, price, context, quality) and synthesizes them into three composite scores: joules/dollars per correct answer, and endpoint fidelity.
AI.motivation: Current AI inference benchmarks compare at model/provider levels, but deployment decisions are made at the endpoint level (provider+model+SKU+quantization+decoding+region+serving stack). Different endpoints serving the same model can vary significantly in performance metrics.
AI.method: Introduces TokenArena framework that measures inference at endpoint granularity across five core axes: output speed, time to first token, workload-blended price, effective context, and quality on live endpoints. Combines these with modeled energy estimates into three composite scores.
AI.result: Across 78 endpoints serving 12 model families: same model on different endpoints differs by up to 12.5 points in accuracy, 12 points in fidelity similarity, order of magnitude in tail latency, and 6.2x in modeled joules per correct answer. Workload-aware pricing substantially reorders rankings - 7 of top 10 chat endpoints fall out under retrieval-augmented preset.
AI.conclusion: TokenArena provides a more granular benchmarking methodology for AI inference endpoints, revealing significant performance variations between different endpoint configurations of the same model. The framework emphasizes workload-aware evaluation and is released as open methodology with full provenance.

title: CRADIPOR: Crash Dispersion Predictor
summary:
We present CRADIPOR, a numerical dispersion prediction tool for automotive crash simulations. Finite Element (FE) crash models are widely used throughout vehicle development, but their predictions are not strictly repeatable because of parallel computation and model complexity. As a result, performance criteria evaluated during post-processing may exhibit significant numerical dispersion, which complicates engineering decision-making. Although dispersion can be estimated by repeating the same simulation, this approach is generally impractical because of its high computational cost.
  This work therefore investigates a prediction tool that can be applied during routine crash-simulation post-processing without repeating the computation. The proposed approach relies on a Rank Reduction Autoencoder (RRAE) combined with supervised classification in order to identify regions sensitive to numerical dispersion. The comparative analysis suggests that the RRAE-based framework is more effective than the Random Forest baseline on the studied dataset. Among the tested signal representations, wavelet-based and slope-based inputs appear to be the most promising, with slope variations providing the best classification performance. These results support the use of structured latent representations for improving numerical-dispersion detection in automotive crash post-processing.

AI.tldr: CRADIPOR is a numerical dispersion prediction tool for automotive crash simulations that uses Rank Reduction Autoencoder with supervised classification to identify dispersion-sensitive regions without repeating simulations.
AI.motivation: FE crash models in vehicle development suffer from numerical dispersion due to parallel computation and complexity, making predictions non-repeatable and complicating engineering decisions. Current methods requiring repeated simulations are computationally impractical.
AI.method: Proposes a Rank Reduction Autoencoder (RRAE) combined with supervised classification to identify regions sensitive to numerical dispersion during routine post-processing. Tests wavelet-based and slope-based signal representations for input data.
AI.result: RRAE-based framework outperforms Random Forest baseline on studied dataset. Wavelet-based and slope-based inputs show promise, with slope variations providing best classification performance for dispersion detection.
AI.conclusion: The results support using structured latent representations (like RRAE) to improve numerical-dispersion detection in automotive crash post-processing, offering a practical alternative to computationally expensive repeated simulations.

title: AgentFloor: How Far Up the tool use Ladder Can Small Open-Weight Models Go?
summary: Production agentic systems make many model calls per user request, and most of those calls are short, structured, and routine. This raises a practical routing question that existing evaluations do not directly answer: which parts of an agent workflow truly require large frontier intelligence, and which can be handled by smaller models? We introduce AgentFloor, a deterministic 30-task benchmark organized as a six-tier capability ladder, spanning instruction following, tool use, multi-step coordination, and long-horizon planning under persistent constraints. We evaluate 16 open-weight models, from 0.27B to 32B parameters, alongside GPT-5 across 16,542 scored runs. Our results reveal a clear boundary of model necessity. Small and mid-sized open-weight models are already sufficient for much of the short-horizon, structured tool use work that dominates real agent pipelines, and in aggregate, the strongest open-weight model matches GPT-5 on our benchmark while being substantially cheaper and faster to run. The gap appears most clearly on long-horizon planning tasks that require sustained coordination and reliable constraint tracking over many steps, where frontier models still hold an advantage, though neither side reaches strong reliability. We also find that this boundary is not explained by scale alone: some failures respond to targeted interventions, but the effects are model-specific rather than universal. These findings suggest a practical design principle for agentic systems: use smaller open-weight models for the broad base of routine actions, and reserve large frontier models for the narrower class of tasks that truly demand deeper planning and control. We release the benchmark, harness, sweep configurations, and full run corpus.

AI.tldr: AgentFloor benchmark shows smaller open-weight models are sufficient for most routine agent tasks, while frontier models excel at complex planning tasks, suggesting cost-effective agent system design strategies.
AI.motivation: Production agent systems make many model calls per request, mostly short/structured/routine calls, raising the practical question of which parts truly need large frontier models vs smaller models - a question existing evaluations don't answer.
AI.method: Introduce AgentFloor, a deterministic 30-task benchmark organized as six-tier capability ladder spanning instruction following, tool use, multi-step coordination, and long-horizon planning under persistent constraints. Evaluate 16 open-weight models (0.27B to 32B parameters) alongside GPT-5 across 16,542 scored runs.
AI.result: Clear boundary of model necessity: small/mid-sized open-weight models sufficient for most short-horizon, structured tool use dominating real agent pipelines. Strongest open-weight model matches GPT-5 on benchmark while being cheaper/faster. Gap appears on long-horizon planning requiring sustained coordination and constraint tracking, where frontier models still hold advantage. Boundary not explained by scale alone - failures respond to targeted interventions but effects are model-specific.
AI.conclusion: Practical design principle for agentic systems: use smaller open-weight models for routine actions, reserve large frontier models for tasks requiring deeper planning and control. Release benchmark, harness, configurations, and full run corpus.

title: Hyperspherical Forward-Forward with Prototypical Representations
summary: The Forward-Forward (FF) algorithm presents a compelling, bio-inspired alternative to backpropagation. However, while efficient in training, it has a computationally prohibitive inference process that requires a separate forward pass for every class that is evaluated. In this work, we introduce the Hyperspherical Forward-Forward (HFF), a novel reformulation that resolves this critical bottleneck. Our core innovation is to reframe the local objective of each layer from a binary goodness-of-fit task to a direct multi-class classification problem within a hyperspherical feature space. We achieve this by learning a set of class-specific, unit-norm prototypes that act as geometric anchors and implicit negatives. This architectural innovation preserves the benefits of local training while enabling weight update and inference in a single forward pass, making it >40x faster than the original FF algorithm. Our method is simple to implement, scales effectively to modern convolutional architectures, and achieves superior accuracy on standard image classification benchmarks, closing the gap with backpropagation. Most notably, we are among the first greedy local-learning methods to report over 25% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1k, and 65.96% with transfer learning.

AI.tldr: HFF improves the Forward-Forward algorithm by making inference >40x faster with hyperspherical prototypes, enabling single-pass inference and competitive performance on ImageNet.
AI.motivation: The Forward-Forward algorithm has a computationally prohibitive inference process that requires separate forward passes for every class, making it impractical for real-world applications.
AI.method: Reformulates the local objective from binary goodness-of-fit to direct multi-class classification using hyperspherical prototypes - learning class-specific unit-norm prototypes that serve as geometric anchors and implicit negatives.
