AI.conclusion: Polaris successfully decouples semantic and hierarchical representations using hyperspherical geometry, enabling more effective learning of real-world knowledge hierarchies with substantial performance gains across diverse hierarchical structures.

title: Caracal: Causal Architecture via Spectral Mixing
summary: The scalability of Large Language Models to long sequences is hindered by the quadratic cost of attention and the limitations of positional encodings. To address these, we introduce Caracal, a novel architecture that replaces attention with a parameter-efficient, $\mathcal{O}(L \log L)$ Multi-Head Fourier (MHF) module. Our contributions are threefold: (1) We leverage the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) for sequence mixing, inherently addressing both bottlenecks mentioned above. (2) We apply a frequency-domain causal masking technique that enforces autoregressive capabilities via asymmetric padding and truncation, overcoming a critical barrier for Fourier-based generative models. (3) Unlike efficient models relying on hardware-specific implementations (e.g., Mamba), we uses standard library operators. This ensures robust portability, eliminating common deployment barriers. Evaluations demonstrate that Caracal performs competitively with Transformer and SSM baselines, offering a scalable and simple pathway for efficient long-sequence modeling. Code is available in Appendix.

AI.tldr: Caracal replaces attention with O(L log L) Multi-Head Fourier module using FFT for efficient long-sequence modeling with causal masking and standard operators for portability.
AI.motivation: Scalability issues of LLMs due to quadratic attention cost and positional encoding limitations hinder long-sequence modeling.
AI.method: Introduces Caracal architecture with Multi-Head Fourier (MHF) module leveraging Fast Fourier Transform for sequence mixing, frequency-domain causal masking via asymmetric padding/truncation, and uses standard library operators for portability.
AI.result: Caracal performs competitively with Transformer and SSM baselines while offering scalable and simple pathway for efficient long-sequence modeling.
AI.conclusion: Caracal addresses scalability bottlenecks of attention and positional encodings through Fourier-based approach with O(L log L) complexity, providing a portable solution for efficient long-sequence modeling.

title: Odysseus: Scaling VLMs to 100+ Turn Decision-Making in Games via Reinforcement Learning
summary: Given the rapidly growing capabilities of vision-language models (VLMs), extending them to interactive decision-making tasks such as video games has emerged as a promising frontier. However, existing approaches either rely on large-scale supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on human trajectories or apply reinforcement learning (RL) only in relatively short-horizon settings (typically around 20--30 turns). In this work, we study RL-based training of VLMs for long-horizon decision-making in Super Mario Land, a visually grounded environment requiring 100+ turns of interaction with coordinated perception, reasoning, and action. We begin with a systematic investigation of key algorithmic components and propose an adapted variant of PPO with a lightweight turn-level critic, which substantially improves training stability and sample efficiency over critic-free methods such as GRPO and Reinforce++. We further show that pretrained VLMs provide strong action priors, significantly improving sample efficiency during RL training and reducing the need for manual design choices such as action engineering, compared to classical deep RL trained from scratch. Building on these insights, we introduce Odysseus, an open training framework for VLM agents, achieving substantial gains across multiple levels of the game and at least 3 times average game progresses than frontier models. Moreover, the trained models exhibit consistent improvements under both in-game and cross-game generalization settings, while maintaining general-domain capabilities. Overall, our results identify key ingredients for making RL stable and effective in long-horizon, multi-modal settings, and provide practical guidance for developing VLMs as embodied agents.

AI.tldr: Using RL with turn-level critic and pretrained VLM priors enables stable training for long-horizon decision-making in visually grounded games like Super Mario Land.
AI.motivation: Extend vision-language models to interactive decision-making tasks in visually complex, long-horizon environments where existing approaches either need extensive human supervision or work only in short-horizon settings.
AI.method: Adapted PPO with lightweight turn-level critic for stability, leverages pretrained VLMs as action priors instead of training from scratch, introduces Odysseus framework for VLM agents.
AI.result: Achieves substantial gains across game levels with 3x+ average progress vs frontier models, shows consistent improvements in in-game/cross-game generalization while maintaining general-domain capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Identifies key ingredients for stable RL in long-horizon multimodal settings, provides practical guidance for developing VLMs as embodied agents.

title: Hypergraph and Latent ODE Learning for Multimodal Root Cause Localization in Microservices
summary: Root cause localization in cloud native microservice systems requires modeling complex service dependencies, irregular temporal dynamics, and heterogeneous observability data. We present HyperODE RCA, a unified framework that combines hypergraph attention learning, latent ordinary differential equations, and multimodal cross attention fusion for fine grained root cause analysis. The method learns higher order service interactions through differentiable hyperedge construction, captures continuous anomaly evolution from irregular observations with an ODE RNN encoder, and adaptively fuses logs, traces, metrics, entities, and events using context aware modality routing. We further improve robustness with a variational information bottleneck, temporal causal regularization, and invariant risk constraints. Experiments on the Tianchi AIOps benchmark show clear gains over strong baselines in ranking and classification performance, while preserving interpretability through learned hypergraph attention.

AI.tldr: HyperODE RCA is a unified framework combining hypergraph attention, ODE modeling, and multimodal fusion for root cause analysis in microservice systems.
AI.motivation: Need to address challenges in root cause localization for cloud native microservice systems, including complex service dependencies, irregular temporal dynamics, and heterogeneous observability data.
AI.method: Combines hypergraph attention learning, latent ordinary differential equations, and multimodal cross attention fusion, with robustness enhancements from variational information bottleneck, temporal causal regularization, and invariant risk constraints.
AI.result: Experiments on Tianchi AIOps benchmark show clear gains over strong baselines in ranking and classification performance.
AI.conclusion: HyperODE RCA is a robust framework for root cause analysis that preserves interpretability through learned hypergraph attention and shows clear performance gains on benchmark datasets.

title: A Dirac-Frenkel-Onsager principle: Instantaneous residual minimization with gauge momentum for nonlinear parametrizations of PDE solutions
summary:
Dirac-Frenkel instantaneous residual minimization evolves nonlinear parametrizations of PDE solutions in time, but ill-conditioning can render the parameter dynamics non-unique.
  We interpret this non-uniqueness as a gauge freedom: nullspace directions that leave the
  time derivative unchanged can be used to select better-conditioned parameter velocities.
  Building on Onsager's minimum-dissipation principle, we introduce a history variable -- interpretable as momentum -- and inject it only along the nullspace directions.
  The resulting Dirac-Frenkel-Onsager dynamics preserve instantaneous residual minimization, in contrast to standard regularization that can introduce bias, while promoting temporally smooth parameter evolutions.
  Examples demonstrate that the approach leads to increased robustness in singular and near-singular regimes.

AI.tldr: Dirac-Frenkel-Onsager dynamics uses gauge freedom from nullspace directions to address ill-conditioning in nonlinear PDE parametrizations, preserving residual minimization while promoting smooth parameter evolution.
AI.motivation: Dirac-Frenkel instantaneous residual minimization for nonlinear PDE parametrizations suffers from ill-conditioning that can render parameter dynamics non-unique, creating robustness issues in singular/near-singular regimes.
AI.method: Interpret non-uniqueness as gauge freedom; use nullspace directions to select better-conditioned parameter velocities. Introduce history variable (interpretable as momentum) based on Onsager's minimum-dissipation principle, injecting it only along nullspace directions.
AI.result: Dirac-Frenkel-Onsager dynamics preserve instantaneous residual minimization (avoiding bias from standard regularization) while promoting temporally smooth parameter evolutions. Examples demonstrate increased robustness in singular and near-singular regimes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Dirac-Frenkel-Onsager dynamics effectively addresses ill-conditioning issues in nonlinear PDE parametrizations by leveraging gauge freedom and momentum injection along nullspace directions, maintaining accuracy while improving robustness.

title: VQ-SAD: Vector Quantized Structure Aware Diffusion For Molecule Generation
summary: Many diffusion based molecule generation methods ignore the symbolic information of molecules and represent the atom and bond type as one hot representation. Methods based on Morgan fingerprints produce hash collisions and are hard to embed into a continuous space without information loss and random fingerprints correspond to no valid molecule. To circumvent this issue we use another paradigm and consider atom and bond codes as latent variables of VQ-VAE. We introduce VQ-SAD which first trains a VQ-VAE and uses the frozen pretrained VQ-VAE model and considers the codebooks for both atom and bond types as tokenizers for the downstream diffusion process. VQ-SAD is a neuro-symbolic model that utilizes both symbolic and neural structural information for a diffusion based model with learnable forward process. The large discrete code space provides a more balanced atom and bond types which enhances the denoising process. VQ-VAE slightly outperforms SOTA models for diffusion based molecule generation on QM9 and ZINC250k datasets.

AI.tldr: VQ-SAD: A neuro-symbolic diffusion model for molecule generation that uses VQ-VAE codebooks as tokenizers for atom and bond types, combining symbolic and neural information.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion-based molecule generation methods either ignore symbolic information (using one-hot representations) or face issues with fingerprint-based approaches (hash collisions, embedding difficulties, invalid molecules from random fingerprints). There's a need to better incorporate symbolic structural information into diffusion models.
AI.method: Train a VQ-VAE first, then use its frozen pretrained codebooks for atom and bond types as tokenizers in a downstream diffusion process. The model (VQ-SAD) is neuro-symbolic and uses both symbolic and neural structural information with a learnable forward process.
AI.result: The large discrete code space provides more balanced atom and bond types which enhances the denoising process. VQ-SAD slightly outperforms state-of-the-art models for diffusion-based molecule generation on QM9 and ZINC250k datasets.
AI.conclusion: Using VQ-VAE codebooks as tokenizers for atom and bond types effectively combines symbolic and neural information for molecule generation, addressing limitations of existing approaches and achieving improved performance on benchmark datasets.

title: AlphaInventory: Evolving White-Box Inventory Policies via Large Language Models with Deployment Guarantees
summary: We study how large language models can be used to evolve inventory policies in online, non-stationary environments. Our work is motivated by recent advances in LLM-based evolutionary search, such as AlphaEvolve, which demonstrates strong performance for static and highly structured problems such as mathematical discovery, but is not directly suited to online dynamic inventory settings. To this end, we propose AlphaInventory, an end-to-end inventory-policy evolution and inference framework grounded in confidence-interval-based certification. The framework trains a large language model using reinforcement learning, incorporates demand data as well as numerical and textual features beyond demand, and generates white-box inventory policy with statistical safety guarantees for deployment in future periods. We further introduce a unified theoretical interface that connects training, inference, and deployment. This allows us to characterize the probability that the AlphaInventory evolves a statistically safe and improved policy, and to quantify the deployment gap relative to the oracle-safe benchmark. Tested on both synthetic data and real-world retail data, AlphaInventory outperforms classical inventory policies and deep learning based methods. In canonical inventory settings, it evolves new policies that improve upon existing benchmarks.

AI.tldr: AlphaInventory: An LLM-based framework for evolving inventory policies with statistical safety guarantees in online non-stationary environments, outperforming classical and deep learning methods.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based evolutionary approaches like AlphaEvolve work well for static, structured problems but are not suitable for online dynamic inventory settings where policies need to adapt to non-stationary environments with statistical safety guarantees.
AI.method: Proposes AlphaInventory - an end-to-end framework that trains LLMs using reinforcement learning, incorporates demand data plus numerical/textual features, generates white-box policies with statistical safety guarantees via confidence-interval-based certification, and provides a unified theoretical interface connecting training, inference, and deployment.
AI.result: Outperforms classical inventory policies and deep learning methods on both synthetic and real-world retail data. Evolves new policies that improve upon existing benchmarks in canonical inventory settings, with quantified safety and performance guarantees.
AI.conclusion: AlphaInventory successfully adapts LLM-based evolutionary search to dynamic inventory problems, providing statistically safe policies with performance improvements over existing methods, demonstrating the potential of LLMs for complex, non-stationary decision-making problems.

title: Data Deletion Can Help in Adaptive RL
summary: Deploying reinforcement learning policies in the real world requires adapting to time-varying environments. We study this problem in the contextual Markov Decision Process (cMDP) framework, where a family of environments is indexed by a low-dimensional context unknown at test time. The standard approach decomposes the problem: train a so-called "universal policy" which assumes knowledge of the true context, then pair it with a context estimator which approximates context using the observed trajectory. We identify a simple, counterintuitive trick that substantially improves the estimator: randomly delete a fraction of the training buffer after each round. This works because data is collected across multiple rounds using progressively better policies, and older trajectories come from a different distribution than what the estimator will face at deployment time; random deletion creates an implicit exponential decay on older data while preserving diversity without requiring any explicit identification of which samples are stale. This reduces robustness gap by 30% for MLPs and by 6% on average for recurrent networks. Strikingly, it allows a narrow MLP with 5x fewer parameters to outperform a wide MLP trained without deletion. To understand when and why deletion helps, we analyze regularized empirical risk minimization with a mismatch between the train distribution and the distribution at deployment; in this idealized setting, we prove that removing a single uniformly random training point decreases expected test loss in expectation under mild conditions. For ridge regression we make this quantitative: deletion helps when the regularization coefficient is moderate and the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) is sufficiently low, and, crucially, this SNR threshold gives a direct measure of how large the distribution mismatch between training and deployment must be for deletion to be beneficial.

AI.tldr: A simple but effective technique of randomly deleting a fraction of training buffer data improves context estimation for RL deployment in varying environments by reducing distribution mismatch between stale training data and deployment conditions.
AI.motivation: Deploying RL policies in real-world time-varying environments requires adapting to unknown contexts at test time. The standard approach uses a universal policy paired with a context estimator, but older training trajectories come from different distributions than what estimators face at deployment.
AI.method: Introduce random deletion of a fraction of training buffer after each round. This creates implicit exponential decay on older data while preserving diversity without explicit stale sample identification. Also analyze regularized empirical risk minimization with distribution mismatch, proving theoretical benefits of random deletion.
AI.result: Random deletion reduces robustness gap by 30% for MLPs and 6% on average for recurrent networks. It allows a narrow MLP with 5x fewer parameters to outperform a wide MLP trained without deletion. Theoretical analysis shows deletion helps when regularization is moderate and SNR is low, with SNR threshold indicating required distribution mismatch.
AI.conclusion: Random deletion of training buffer data is a simple yet powerful technique to improve context estimation in RL deployment by addressing distribution mismatch between stale training data and deployment conditions. The method offers practical benefits and theoretical insights for when such deletion helps.

title: Trees to Flows and Back: Unifying Decision Trees and Diffusion Models
comment: 12 pages (main), 68 pages (inclusive of appendix), Accepted in the Forty-Third International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML) 2026
summary: Decision trees and diffusion models are ostensibly disparate model classes, one discrete and hierarchical, the other continuous and dynamic. This work unifies the two by establishing a crisp mathematical correspondence between hierarchical decision trees and diffusion processes in appropriate limiting regimes. Our unification reveals a shared optimization principle: \emph{Global Trajectory Score Matching (GTSM)}, for which gradient boosting (in an idealized version) is asymptotically optimal. We underscore the conceptual value of our work through two key practical instantiations: \treeflow, which achieves competitive generation quality on tabular data with higher fidelity and a 2\times computational speedup, and \dsmtree, a novel distillation method that transfers hierarchical decision logic into neural networks, matching teacher performance within 2\% on many benchmarks.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a mathematical unification between decision trees and diffusion models, revealing a shared optimization principle called Global Trajectory Score Matching (GTSM), and demonstrates two practical applications with improved performance.
AI.motivation: Decision trees and diffusion models appear fundamentally different - one is discrete/hierarchical while the other is continuous/dynamic. The authors aim to discover connections between these disparate model classes and leverage this understanding for practical improvements.
AI.method: The paper establishes a mathematical correspondence between hierarchical decision trees and diffusion processes in appropriate limiting regimes. This unification reveals a shared optimization principle: Global Trajectory Score Matching (GTSM), for which gradient boosting becomes asymptotically optimal in an idealized version.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates two key practical instantiations: 1) TreeFlow, which achieves competitive generation quality on tabular data with higher fidelity and 2× computational speedup, and 2) DSMTree, a novel distillation method that transfers hierarchical decision logic into neural networks, matching teacher performance within 2% on many benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The work successfully unifies decision trees and diffusion models through mathematical correspondence, revealing a shared optimization principle that enables practical improvements in both generative modeling (TreeFlow) and knowledge distillation (DSMTree), bridging the gap between discrete hierarchical models and continuous diffusion processes.

title: Federated Weather Modeling on Sensor Data
comment: Accepted by Encyclopedia of GIS, this is an unedited version. Published version: https://link.springer.com/rwe/10.1007/978-3-319-23519-6_1719-1
summary: Federated weather modeling on sensor data is a distributed system underpinned by federated learning, enabling multiple sensor data sources, including ground weather stations, satellites and IoT devices, to collaboratively train deep learning models without sharing raw data. This method safeguards data privacy and security while leverages diverse, geographically distributed datasets to improve the accuracy and robustness of global/regional weather modeling tasks such as forecasting and anomaly detection.

AI.tldr: Federated learning enables collaborative weather modeling across distributed sensors without sharing raw data, improving accuracy while maintaining privacy.
AI.motivation: Weather modeling requires diverse, geographically distributed data from multiple sources (ground stations, satellites, IoT devices), but sharing raw data raises privacy and security concerns. There's a need to leverage distributed datasets while protecting sensitive information.
AI.method: Federated learning is employed as a distributed system where multiple sensor data sources collaboratively train deep learning models without exchanging raw data. This involves training models locally on each data source and aggregating model updates rather than data itself.
AI.result: The approach safeguards data privacy and security while leveraging diverse datasets to improve the accuracy and robustness of global/regional weather modeling tasks such as forecasting and anomaly detection.
AI.conclusion: Federated weather modeling provides a practical solution for collaborative weather prediction that addresses privacy concerns, enabling more accurate and robust models through distributed learning across multiple data sources while keeping sensitive data localized.

title: BWLA: Breaking the Barrier of W1AX Post-Training Quantization for LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have driven major progress in NLP, yet their substantial memory and compute demands still hinder practical deployment. Binarization can compress weights to 1 bit, fundamentally lowering compute and bandwidth cost. However, existing methods cannot address activation heavy tails and thus must keep activations in high precision, preventing true end-to-end acceleration. To overcome this limitation, we propose BWLA (Binarized Weights and Low-bit Activations), the first post-training quantization framework that preserves high accuracy while achieving 1-bit weight quantization together with low-bit activations (e.g., 6 bits). The Orthogonal-Kronecker Transformation (OKT) learns an orthogonal mapping via EM minimization, converting unimodal weights into symmetric bimodal forms while suppressing activation tails and incoherence. The Proximal SVD Projection (PSP) then performs lightweight low-rank refinement through proximal SVD projection, further enhancing quantizability with minimal overhead. On Qwen3-32B, BWLA reaches a Wikitext2 perplexity of 11.92 under 6-bit activations (vs. 38 from SOTA), improves five zero-shot tasks by more than 70%, and delivers 3.26 times inference speedup, demonstrating strong potential for real-world LLM compression and acceleration.

AI.tldr: BWLA is a post-training quantization framework that achieves 1-bit weight binarization with low-bit activations (e.g., 6 bits) through Orthogonal-Kronecker Transformation and Proximal SVD Projection, enabling practical LLM compression with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: LLMs have significant memory and compute demands that hinder practical deployment. While binarization can compress weights to 1 bit, existing methods cannot handle activation heavy tails and must keep activations in high precision, preventing true end-to-end acceleration.
AI.method: BWLA combines two key techniques: 1) Orthogonal-Kronecker Transformation (OKT) that learns an orthogonal mapping via EM minimization to convert unimodal weights into symmetric bimodal forms while suppressing activation tails and incoherence. 2) Proximal SVD Projection (PSP) that performs lightweight low-rank refinement through proximal SVD projection to enhance quantizability with minimal overhead.
AI.result: On Qwen3-32B, BWLA achieves Wikitext2 perplexity of 11.92 under 6-bit activations (vs. 38 from SOTA), improves five zero-shot tasks by more than 70%, and delivers 3.26 times inference speedup.
AI.conclusion: BWLA demonstrates strong potential for real-world LLM compression and acceleration by preserving high accuracy while enabling true end-to-end quantization with 1-bit weights and low-bit activations.

title: Conformalized Quantum DeepONet Ensembles for Scalable Operator Learning with Distribution-Free Uncertainty
summary: Operator learning enables fast surrogate modeling of high-dimensional dynamical systems, but existing approaches face two fundamental limitations: quadratic inference complexity and unreliable uncertainty quantification in safety-critical settings. We propose Conformalized Quantum DeepONet Ensembles, a framework that addresses both challenges simultaneously. By leveraging Quantum Orthogonal Neural Networks (QOrthoNNs), we reduce operator inference complexity from O(n^2) to O(n), enabling scalable evaluation over fine discretizations. To provide rigorous uncertainty quantification, we combine ensemble-based epistemic modeling with adaptive conformal prediction, yielding distribution-free coverage guarantees. A key challenge in ensembling is that naive parallelism scales hardware resources linearly with the number of models. We resolve this by using Superposed Parameterized Quantum Circuits (SPQCs), which compress multiple ensemble members into a single circuit and enable simultaneous multi-model execution. Experiments on synthetic partial differential equations and real-world power system dynamics demonstrate that our approach achieves accurate predictions while maintaining calibrated uncertainty under realistic quantum noise. These results establish a practical pathway toward scalable, uncertainty-aware operator learning in quantum machine learning.

AI.tldr: A framework combining Quantum Orthogonal Neural Networks and conformal prediction reduces operator inference complexity and provides rigorous uncertainty quantification, enabling scalable, uncertainty-aware operator learning in quantum machine learning.
AI.motivation: Existing operator learning approaches suffer from quadratic inference complexity and unreliable uncertainty quantification in safety-critical settings, limiting their scalability and reliability.
AI.method: The method leverages Quantum Orthogonal Neural Networks (QOrthoNNs) to reduce inference complexity from O(n^2) to O(n), uses ensemble-based epistemic modeling combined with adaptive conformal prediction for uncertainty quantification, and employs Superposed Parameterized Quantum Circuits (SPQCs) to compress multiple ensemble members into a single circuit for simultaneous multi-model execution.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic partial differential equations and real-world power system dynamics show that the approach achieves accurate predictions while maintaining calibrated uncertainty under realistic quantum noise.
AI.conclusion: Conformalized Quantum DeepONet Ensembles provide a scalable and uncertainty-aware framework for operator learning, achieving accurate predictions with calibrated uncertainty under quantum noise, and establish a practical pathway for quantum machine learning in safety-critical applications.

title: PAMod: Modeling Cyclical Shifts via Phase-Amplitude Modulation for Non-stationary Time Series Forecasting
summary: Real-world time series forecasting faces the fundamental challenge of non-stationary statistical properties, including shifts in mean and variance over time. While reversible instance normalization (RevIN) has shown promise by stationarizing inputs and denormalizing outputs, it relies on the strong assumption that historical and future distributions remain identical. We observe that in many practical applications, distribution shifts follow cyclical patterns that correlate with periodic positions (e.g., seasonal and holiday volatility). To this end, we propose PAMod, a lightweight yet powerful framework that models cyclical distribution shifts via Phase-Amplitude Modulation in the normalized feature space. PAMod learns periodic embeddings to modulate representations: phase modulation captures mean shifts, while amplitude modulation adapts to variance changes. Crucially, we prove mathematically that modulating in normalized space is equivalent to applying dynamic denormalization, offering an elegant unification of distribution adaptation and representation learning. Extensive experiments on twelve real-world benchmarks demonstrate that PAMod achieves state-of-the-art performance with fewer computational resources. Furthermore, our modulation mechanism, as a novel plug-and-play technique, can improve existing time-series forecasting methods with simple integration.

AI.tldr: PAMod is a lightweight framework that uses phase-amplitude modulation to address cyclical distribution shifts in time series forecasting, outperforming existing methods while being computationally efficient.
AI.motivation: Real-world time series forecasting faces non-stationary statistical properties like shifts in mean and variance. While reversible instance normalization (RevIN) helps by stationarizing inputs, it assumes identical historical and future distributions. However, many applications have cyclical distribution shifts correlated with periodic positions (e.g., seasonal/holiday volatility), requiring adaptation to these dynamic changes.
AI.method: PAMod models cyclical distribution shifts via Phase-Amplitude Modulation in normalized feature space. It learns periodic embeddings to modulate representations: phase modulation captures mean shifts, while amplitude modulation adapts to variance changes. Crucially, modulation in normalized space is mathematically equivalent to applying dynamic denormalization, unifying distribution adaptation and representation learning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on twelve real-world benchmarks show PAMod achieves state-of-the-art performance with fewer computational resources. The modulation mechanism serves as a novel plug-and-play technique that can improve existing time-series forecasting methods through simple integration.
AI.conclusion: PAMod provides an effective solution for handling cyclical distribution shifts in time series forecasting through phase-amplitude modulation, offering both superior performance and computational efficiency while being easily integrable into existing methods.

title: Borrowed Geometry: Computational Reuse of Frozen Text-Pretrained Transformer Weights Across Modalities
summary: Frozen Gemma 4 31B weights pretrained exclusively on text tokens, unmodified, transfer across modality boundaries through a thin trainable interface. (1) OGBench scene-play-singletask-task1-v0: $+4.33$pt over published GCIQL at $n=3$ with std 0.74 -- a published-SOTA win on a robotic manipulation task the substrate has never seen. (2) D4RL Walker2d-medium-v2: Decision-Transformer parity ($76.2 \pm 0.8$, $n=3$) at $0.43\times$ DT's trainable count, with the frozen substrate compressing to a 5L slice ($+1.66$pt over the 6L baseline at $n=3$). (3) Associative recall as the cleanest pretraining-load-bearing case: the frozen slice + a 113K-parameter linear interface reaches L30 best-checkpoint per-bit error 0.0505 ($n=2$); a 6.36M-parameter from-scratch trained transformer at matched capacity ($1/\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling, two seeds, LR sweep) cannot solve the task at all under the protocol (best L30 = 0.4395), an $8.7\times$ advantage. Architecture-alone falsifications: a frozen random transformer with correct $1/\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling stays at random-chance loss for 50k steps; a random-init Gemma slice fails OGBench cube-double-play-task1 entirely (0.89% across $n=3$ where pretrained reaches 60%). A dual-measurement protocol -- text-activation probing on 95 English sentences plus task-ablation on a non-language target -- names individual heads independently identifiable on both protocols: head L26.28 scores $3.7\times$ the slice mean for English token-copying and is the #2 most-critical head for binary copy ablation ($Δ$ L30 $= +0.221$); three further heads (L27.28, L27.2, L27.3) classify by the same protocol. The mechanism is single-model and the cross-modality results are single-task within their respective benchmarks; cross-model replication is structurally constrained because Gemma 4 31B is the only model on the small-scale Pareto frontier as of April 2026.

AI.tldr: Frozen Gemma 4 31B pretrained exclusively on text tokens successfully transfers across modalities through a thin trainable interface, achieving SOTA robotic manipulation results, decision transformer parity with fewer parameters, and significant advantages in associative recall tasks.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate that large language models pretrained on text-only data can transfer their capabilities to non-language domains (robotics, locomotion, associative recall) through minimal modification, challenging the conventional belief that cross-modal transfer requires extensive multimodal training.
AI.method: Using frozen Gemma 4 31B weights pretrained on text tokens, with only a thin trainable interface (linear layers) added for specific tasks. Testing across three domains: (1) robotic manipulation (OGBench), (2) locomotion (D4RL Walker2d), (3) associative recall. Comparing against from-scratch transformers and architectures with random initialization.
AI.result: (1) +4.33pt improvement over published GCIQL on robotic manipulation task (SOTA win). (2) Decision Transformer parity (76.2±0.8) with only 0.43× trainable parameters, and frozen substrate compressing to 5L slice (+1.66pt over 6L baseline). (3) Frozen slice + 113K-parameter linear interface achieves L30 per-bit error 0.0505 in associative recall, while 6.36M-parameter from-scratch transformer fails (best L30=0.4395), showing 8.7× advantage. Architecture-alone falsifications confirm pretraining is essential.
AI.conclusion: Text-only pretrained LLMs can effectively transfer across modality boundaries through minimal interfaces, achieving competitive or superior performance compared to specialized models. This demonstrates that language pretraining creates valuable general representations applicable to non-language tasks, challenging assumptions about cross-modal transfer requirements.

title: Scalable Context-Aware Graph Attention for Unsupervised Anomaly Detection in Large-Scale Mobile Networks
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary:
Mobile network operators must monitor thousands of heterogeneous network elements across the radio access network and the packet core, each exposing high-dimensional KPI time series. The scale and cost of incident labelling make supervised approaches impractical, motivating unsupervised anomaly detection robust to context shifts and nonstationarity.
  We propose \textbf{C-MTAD-GAT} (\emph{Context-aware Multivariate Time-series Anomaly Detection with Graph Attention}), an anomaly detection framework designed to operate as a single shared model across large populations of network elements. The model combines temporal and feature-wise graph attention with lightweight static and dynamic context conditioning and a dual-head decoder for reconstruction and multi-step forecasting. It produces per-element, per-feature anomaly scores, converted to alerts via fully unsupervised thresholds calibrated from validation residuals.
  On the TELCO dataset released with DC-VAE \cite{garcia2023onemodel}, C-MTAD-GAT improves event-level affiliation and pointwise F1 while generating fewer alarms than prior graph-attention and VAE-based baselines. We then apply the same system to nation-scale radio access and evolved packet core control-plane counter data from a mobile network operator, where it is deployed. Operator feedback indicates the alerts are actionable and support daily monitoring, showing scalability across domains without relying on labelled incidents.

AI.tldr: Proposes C-MTAD-GAT, an unsupervised anomaly detection framework for telecom networks using graph attention and context conditioning, showing improved performance and scalability in real-world deployment.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the impracticality of supervised anomaly detection in large-scale mobile networks due to high labeling costs and the need for robustness against context shifts and nonstationarity in high-dimensional KPI time series.
AI.method: The method involves a context-aware multivariate time-series anomaly detection model combining temporal and feature-wise graph attention, lightweight static/dynamic context conditioning, and a dual-head decoder for reconstruction and multi-step forecasting. It uses unsupervised threshold calibration from validation residuals to generate per-element, per-feature anomaly scores.
AI.result: On the TELCO dataset, C-MTAD-GAT improves event-level affiliation and pointwise F1 while generating fewer alarms than prior graph-attention and VAE-based baselines. In a nation-scale deployment, operator feedback indicates the alerts are actionable and support daily monitoring.
AI.conclusion: The C-MTAD-GAT framework provides a scalable, unsupervised anomaly detection solution that effectively handles large-scale, heterogeneous network monitoring with minimal labeling requirements, demonstrating practical utility in real-world telecom deployments.

title: Free Energy Surface Sampling via Reduced Flow Matching
summary: Sampling the free energy surface, namely, the distribution of collective variables (CVs), is a crucial problem in statistical physics, as it underpins a better understanding of chemical reactions and conformational transitions. Traditional methods for free energy surface sampling involve simulation in high-dimensional configuration space and projecting the resulting configurations onto the CV space. To reduce the computational costs of such sampling, we propose FES-FM, a reduced flow matching (FM) method for free energy sampling (FES). We train a dynamical transport map in the CV space, thereby enabling direct sampling of the free energy surface. For many-particle systems, we construct a prior distribution based on the Hessian at a local minimum of the potential, which ensures both rotation-translation invariance and physically meaningful configurations. We evaluate the proposed method across a variety of potential functions and collective variables. Comparative experiments demonstrate that our approach drastically reduces computational costs while delivering superior accuracy per unit sampling time.

AI.tldr: FES-FM is a reduced flow matching method for free energy sampling that trains dynamical transport maps in collective variable space to directly sample free energy surfaces, reducing computational costs while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Traditional free energy surface sampling methods require expensive simulations in high-dimensional configuration space followed by projection to collective variable space, which is computationally intensive. There is a need to reduce computational costs while maintaining accurate sampling of free energy surfaces.
AI.method: The method uses a reduced flow matching (FM) technique to train a dynamical transport map in collective variable space, enabling direct sampling of the free energy surface. For many-particle systems, it constructs a prior distribution based on the Hessian at local potential minima to ensure rotation-translation invariance and physical meaningfulness of configurations.
AI.result: Comparative experiments across various potential functions and collective variables demonstrate that FES-FM drastically reduces computational costs while delivering superior accuracy per unit sampling time compared to traditional methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed FES-FM method provides an effective alternative to traditional free energy sampling methods by enabling direct sampling of free energy surfaces through a reduced flow matching approach, which offers substantial computational savings while maintaining high accuracy per unit sampling time.

title: Hierarchical Abstract Tree for Cross-Document Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) enhances large language models with external knowledge, and tree-based RAG organizes documents into hierarchical indexes to support queries at multiple granularities. However, existing Tree-RAG methods designed for single-document retrieval face critical challenges in scaling to cross-document multi-hop questions: (1) poor distribution adaptability, where $k$-means clustering introduces noise due to rigid distribution assumptions; (2) structural isolation, as tree indexes lack explicit cross-document connections; and (3) coarse abstraction, which obscures fine-grained details. To address these limitations, we propose $Ψ$-RAG, a tree-RAG framework with two key components. First, a hierarchical abstract tree index built through an iterative "merging and collapse" process that adapts to data distributions without a priori assumption. Second, a multi-granular retrieval agent that intelligently interacts with the knowledge base with reorganized queries and an agent-powered hybrid retriever. $Ψ$-RAG supports diverse tasks from token-level question answering to document-level summarization. On cross-document multi-hop QA benchmarks, it outperforms RAPTOR by 25.9% and HippoRAG 2 by 7.4% in average F1 score. Code is available at https://github.com/Newiz430/Psi-RAG.

AI.tldr: Psi-RAG addresses limitations of traditional Tree-RAG systems for multi-hop cross-document QA by introducing an adaptive hierarchical abstract tree index and multi-granular retrieval agent, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing Tree-RAG methods designed for single-document retrieval face three critical challenges when scaling to cross-document multi-hop questions: poor distribution adaptability (k-means clustering introduces noise due to rigid distribution assumptions), structural isolation (tree indexes lack explicit cross-document connections), and coarse abstraction (which obscures fine-grained details).
AI.method: Psi-RAG proposes two key components: 1) a hierarchical abstract tree index built through an iterative "merging and collapse" process that adapts to data distributions without a priori assumption, and 2) a multi-granular retrieval agent that intelligently interacts with the knowledge base with reorganized queries and an agent-powered hybrid retriever.
AI.result: On cross-document multi-hop QA benchmarks, Psi-RAG outperforms RAPTOR by 25.9% and HippoRAG 2 by 7.4% in average F1 score. The framework supports diverse tasks from token-level question answering to document-level summarization.
AI.conclusion: Psi-RAG successfully addresses the scalability challenges of Tree-RAG for multi-hop cross-document queries through its adaptive hierarchical indexing and intelligent retrieval mechanisms, demonstrating significant performance improvements over existing methods.

title: Beyond Continuity: Simulation-free Reconstruction of Discrete Branching Dynamics from Single-cell Snapshots
summary: Inferring cellular trajectories from destructive snapshots is complicated by the challenges of stochasticity and non-conservative mass dynamics such as cell proliferation and apoptosis. Existing unbalanced Optimal Transport (OT) methods treat mass as a continuous fluid, performing inference at the population level. However, this macroscopic view often fails to capture the discrete, jump-like nature of birth-death events at single-cell resolution, which is essential for understanding lineage branching and fate decisions. We present Unbalanced Schrödinger Bridge (USB), a simulation-free framework for learning underlying dynamics that effectively integrates both stochastic and unbalanced effects which also models the discrete, jump-like birth-death dynamics at single-cell resolution. Theoretically, USB provides a tractable solution to the Branching Schrödinger Bridge (BSB) problem, offering a rigorous microscopic interpretation where individual cells undergo both Brownian motion and discrete birth-death jumps. Technically, the method implements an efficient solver by introducing a simulation-free training objective that effectively scales to high-dimensional omics data. Empirically, we demonstrate on both simulated and real-world datasets that USB not only achieves trajectory reconstruction performance better than or comparable to deterministic baselines but also uniquely enables realistic discrete simulation of birth-death dynamics at single-cell resolution.

AI.tldr: USB is a simulation-free framework that learns cellular trajectories by integrating stochastic transport and discrete birth-death dynamics at single-cell resolution, providing better reconstruction than deterministic methods and enabling realistic simulation of lineage branching.
AI.motivation: Existing unbalanced Optimal Transport methods treat cellular mass as continuous fluid at population level, failing to capture discrete, jump-like birth-death events essential for understanding lineage branching and fate decisions at single-cell resolution.
AI.method: Unbalanced Schrödinger Bridge (USB) framework provides a tractable solution to Branching Schrödinger Bridge problem, modeling individual cells undergoing both Brownian motion and discrete birth-death jumps with a simulation-free training objective that scales to high-dimensional omics data.
AI.result: USB achieves trajectory reconstruction performance better than or comparable to deterministic baselines on both simulated and real-world datasets, and uniquely enables realistic discrete simulation of birth-death dynamics at single-cell resolution.
AI.conclusion: USB successfully addresses limitations of macroscopic continuous models by providing a microscopic interpretation of cellular trajectories that captures both stochastic transport and discrete birth-death dynamics, enabling more accurate understanding of lineage branching and fate decisions.

title: Fairness of Classifiers in the Presence of Constraints between Features
comment: To be published in Proc. CP 2026
summary:
In Machine Learning, an accepted definition of fairness of a decision taken by a classifier is that it should not depend on protected features, such as gender. Unfortunately, when constraints exist between features, such dependencies can be obscured by the constraints.
  To avoid this problem, we propose that a decision be considered fair if it has a fair explanation. We define a fair explanation as a prime-implicant reason for the decision that does not contain any protected feature (where the constraints are taken into account in the definition of prime-implicant). Surprisingly, ignoring constraints can completely change the fairness of a decision (according to this definition) even in the absence of constraints between protected and unprotected features.
  Three possible definitions of fairness of a classifier are that for all its decisions (1) there are only fair explanations, (2) there is at least one fair explanation, or (3) changing protected features does not change the outcome. We identify the relationships between these different definitions of fairness and study the computational complexity of testing fairness of classifiers.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new fairness definition for classifiers based on "fair explanations" - prime-implicant reasons for decisions that exclude protected features, even when constraints exist between features. It examines three fairness definitions and studies their relationships and computational complexity.
AI.motivation: Existing fairness definitions that require decisions to be independent of protected features fail when constraints exist between features, as these constraints can obscure dependencies. Standard approaches may incorrectly classify decisions as fair when they're actually biased due to hidden correlations between features.
AI.method: The paper introduces the concept of "fair explanation" as a prime-implicant reason for a decision that contains no protected features, with constraints properly accounted for in the prime-implicant definition. It proposes three fairness definitions: (1) all explanations must be fair, (2) at least one fair explanation exists, (3) changing protected features doesn't change outcomes. The authors analyze relationships between these definitions and study computational complexity of testing fairness.
AI.result: The research shows that ignoring constraints can completely change the fairness assessment of a decision, even when there are no direct constraints between protected and unprotected features. The paper identifies relationships between the three fairness definitions and investigates the computational complexity of testing whether classifiers satisfy these fairness criteria.
AI.conclusion: Fairness should be defined based on explanations rather than just statistical independence from protected features, especially when constraints exist between features. The paper provides a formal framework for fair explanations and demonstrates that traditional fairness definitions may fail when feature constraints obscure dependencies.

title: Possibilistic Predictive Uncertainty for Deep Learning
summary: Deep neural networks achieve impressive results across diverse applications, yet their overconfidence on unseen inputs necessitates reliable epistemic uncertainty modelling. Existing methods for uncertainty modelling face a fundamental dilemma: Bayesian approaches provide principled estimates but remain computationally prohibitive, while efficient second-order predictors lack rigorous derivations connecting their specific objectives to epistemic uncertainty quantification. To resolve this dilemma, we introduce Dirichlet-approximated possibilistic posterior predictions (DAPPr), a principled framework leveraging possibility theory. We define a possibilistic posterior over parameters, projects this posterior to the prediction space via supremum operators, and approximates the projected posterior using learnable Dirichlet possibility functions. This projection-and-approximation strategy yields a simple training objective with closed-form solutions. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves competitive or superior uncertainty quantification performance compared to state-of-the-art evidential deep learning methods while maintaining both principled derivation and computational efficiency. Code will be available at https://github.com/MaxwellYaoNi/DAPPr.

AI.tldr: DAPPr is a principled framework using possibility theory to model epistemic uncertainty in deep networks, bridging Bayesian rigor with computational efficiency through Dirichlet approximations.
AI.motivation: Existing uncertainty modeling methods face a dilemma: Bayesian approaches are principled but computationally prohibitive, while efficient second-order predictors lack rigorous derivations connecting their objectives to epistemic uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: Uses possibility theory to define a possibilistic posterior over parameters, projects it to prediction space via supremum operators, and approximates the projected posterior with learnable Dirichlet possibility functions. This projection-and-approximation yields simple training objectives with closed-form solutions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show DAPPr achieves competitive or superior uncertainty quantification performance compared to state-of-the-art evidential deep learning methods while maintaining both principled derivation and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: DAPPr introduces a principled yet computationally efficient framework for epistemic uncertainty quantification that bridges the gap between Bayesian rigor and practical efficiency. By using possibility theory and Dirichlet approximations, it achieves competitive uncertainty modeling while maintaining both theoretical foundations and computational practicality.

title: Binomial flows: Denoising and flow matching for discrete ordinal data
summary: Flow-based generative modeling in continuous spaces exploit Tweedie's formula to express the denoiser (learned in training) as a score function (used in sampling). In contrast, this relation has been largely missing in the discrete setting where common approaches focus on learning discrete scores and rates. In this work we close this gap for discrete non-negative ordinal data by introducing Binomial flows. Our framework provides a simple recipe for training a discrete diffusion model which simultaneously denoises, samples, and estimates exact likelihoods. We verify our methodology on synthetic examples and obtain competitive results on real-world data sets.

AI.tldr: Binom flows bridge the gap between continuous and discrete diffusion models for ordinal data by introducing a framework that enables simultaneous denoising, sampling, and exact likelihood estimation.
AI.motivation: In continuous spaces, flow-based generative models use Tweedie's formula to connect learned denoisers with score functions, but this relationship has been missing for discrete settings where approaches focus on learning discrete scores and rates separately.
AI.method: Introduces Binomial flows, a framework for discrete non-negative ordinal data that provides a simple training recipe for discrete diffusion models, enabling the simultaneous learning of denoising, sampling, and exact likelihood estimation capabilities.
AI.result: The methodology was verified on synthetic examples and achieved competitive results on real-world datasets, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed Binomial flows framework.
AI.conclusion: Binomial flows successfully close the gap between continuous and discrete diffusion modeling for ordinal data by providing a unified framework that maintains the beneficial properties of Tweedie's formula relationship in the discrete setting.

title: Learning Multimodal Energy-Based Model with Multimodal Variational Auto-Encoder via MCMC Revision
summary: Energy-based models (EBMs) are a flexible class of deep generative models and are well-suited to capture complex dependencies in multimodal data. However, learning multimodal EBM by maximum likelihood requires Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling in the joint data space, where noise-initialized Langevin dynamics often mixes poorly and fails to discover coherent inter-modal relationships. Multimodal VAEs have made progress in capturing such inter-modal dependencies by introducing a shared latent generator and a joint inference model. However, both the shared latent generator and joint inference model are parameterized as unimodal Gaussian (or Laplace), which severely limits their ability to approximate the complex structure induced by multimodal data. In this work, we study the learning problem of the multimodal EBM, shared latent generator, and joint inference model. We present a learning framework that effectively interweaves their MLE updates with corresponding MCMC refinements in both the data and latent spaces. Specifically, the generator is learned to produce coherent multimodal samples that serve as strong initial states for EBM sampling, while the inference model is learned to provide informative latent initializations for generator posterior sampling. Together, these two models serve as complementary models that enable effective EBM sampling and learning, yielding realistic and coherent multimodal EBM samples. Extensive experiments demonstrate superior performance for multimodal synthesis quality and coherence compared to various baselines. We conduct various analyses and ablation studies to validate the effectiveness and scalability of the proposed multimodal framework.

AI.tldr: A framework for learning multimodal energy-based models (EBMs) that addresses sampling challenges by combining shared latent generators and joint inference models with MCMC refinements in both data and latent spaces, enabling coherent multimodal generation.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal EBMs suffer from poor MCMC sampling in joint data space, while multimodal VAEs use overly simple Gaussian/Laplace parameterizations that fail to capture complex multimodal dependencies, limiting their ability to produce realistic and coherent multimodal samples.
AI.method: Proposes a learning framework that interweaves maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) updates with MCMC refinements in both data and latent spaces. The shared latent generator learns to produce coherent multimodal samples for EBM initialization, while the joint inference model provides informative latent initializations for generator posterior sampling, enabling effective EBM sampling and learning.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior performance in multimodal synthesis quality and coherence compared to various baselines. Extensive experiments, analyses, and ablation studies validate the effectiveness and scalability of the proposed multimodal framework.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively combines multimodal EBM, shared latent generator, and joint inference model through alternating MLE and MCMC updates, enabling realistic and coherent multimodal generation by addressing key sampling challenges in both data and latent spaces.

title: Uniform-Correct Policy Optimization: Breaking RLVR's Indifference to Diversity
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has achieved substantial gains in single-attempt accuracy (Pass@1) on reasoning tasks, yet often suffers from reduced multi-sample coverage (Pass@K), indicating diversity collapse. We identify a structural cause for this degradation: common RLVR objectives, such as GRPO, are indifferent to how probability mass is distributed among correct solutions. Combined with stochastic training dynamics, this indifference induces a self-reinforcing collapse, in which probability mass concentrates on a narrow subset of correct outputs while alternative valid solutions are suppressed. We formalize this collapse mechanism and further characterize the optimal policy structure under two complementary criteria: robustness and entropy-regularized optimality, which identify the Uniform-Correct Policy as uniquely optimal. Motivated by this analysis, we propose Uniform-Correct Policy Optimization (UCPO), a modification to GRPO that adds a conditional uniformity penalty on the policy's distribution over correct solutions. The penalty redistributes gradient signal toward underrepresented correct responses, encouraging uniform allocation of probability mass within the correct set. Across three models (1.5B-7B parameters) and five mathematical reasoning benchmarks, UCPO improves Pass@K and diversity while maintaining competitive Pass@1, achieving up to +10\% absolute improvement on AIME24 at Pass@64 and up to 45\% higher equation-level diversity within the correct set. The code is available at https://github.com/AnamikaLochab/UCPO.

AI.tldr: UCPO fixes diversity collapse in RLVR by making the model distribute probability uniformly over correct solutions, improving Pass@K and diversity without hurting Pass@1.
AI.motivation: RLVR methods like GRPO suffer from diversity collapse (reduced Pass@K) due to indifference to how probability mass is distributed among correct solutions, leading to concentration on a narrow subset and suppression of alternative valid solutions.
AI.method: Proposes Uniform-Correct Policy Optimization (UCPO), a modification to GRPO that adds a conditional uniformity penalty on the policy's distribution over correct solutions, redistributing gradient signal to encourage uniform allocation of probability mass within the correct set.
AI.result: UCPO achieves up to +10% absolute improvement on AIME24 at Pass@64 and up to 45% higher equation-level diversity within the correct set across three models (1.5B-7B parameters) and five mathematical reasoning benchmarks, while maintaining competitive Pass@1 performance.
AI.conclusion: The Uniform-Correct Policy Optimization (UCPO) method effectively addresses the diversity collapse issue in RLVR by enforcing uniform probability distribution over correct solutions, leading to significant improvements in multi-sample coverage and diversity while maintaining strong single-attempt accuracy, making it a promising approach for enhancing reasoning tasks.

title: AdaMeZO: Adam-style Zeroth-Order Optimizer for LLM Fine-tuning Without Maintaining the Moments
summary: Fine-tuning LLMs is necessary for various dedicated downstream tasks, but classic backpropagation-based fine-tuning methods require substantial GPU memory. To this end, a recent work, MeZO, which relies solely on forward passes to fine-tune LLMs, significantly reduces GPU requirements at the cost of slower convergence due to its indifference to loss landscapes. Standard solutions, such as Adam, explore loss landscapes by estimating the first- and second-order moments and storing them in memory to guide the model's movement through dimensions with lower curvature and vice versa. However, directly applying Adam negates MeZO's advantage as it will triple the memory requirement. In light of this, we propose AdaMeZO, a zeroth-order optimizer that leverages Adam-style first- and second-moment estimates without maintaining them in memory. We present a theoretical analysis of AdaMeZO, corroborated by extensive experiments demonstrating AdaMeZO's performance, showing that AdaMeZO can outperform MeZO while requiring up to $70\%$ fewer forward passes. Trajectory visualizations affirm AdaMeZO's ability to adapt to diverse loss landscapes.

AI.tldr: AdaMeZO improves memory-efficient LLM fine-tuning by incorporating Adam-style optimization without memory storage, achieving faster convergence than MeZO.
AI.motivation: Standard backpropagation-based fine-tuning methods require substantial GPU memory, and while MeZO reduces memory requirements, it suffers from slower convergence due to its indifference to loss landscapes. Directly applying Adam to MeZO would triple memory usage, negating MeZO's advantage.
AI.method: The authors propose AdaMeZO, a zeroth-order optimizer that incorporates Adam-style first- and second-moment estimates without storing them in memory. This approach enables efficient adaptation to loss landscapes while avoiding the memory overhead of standard Adam.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that AdaMeZO outperforms MeZO while requiring up to 70% fewer forward passes. Trajectory visualizations confirm AdaMeZO's ability to adapt to diverse loss landscapes.
AI.conclusion: The authors introduce AdaMeZO as a novel zeroth-order optimizer that effectively leverages Adam-style first- and second-moment estimates without storing them in memory. This approach significantly improves upon MeZO's convergence speed while preserving its memory advantage.

title: Reinforcement Learning with Markov Risk Measures and Multipattern Risk Approximation
summary: For a risk-averse finite-horizon Markov Decision Problem, we introduce a special class of Markov coherent risk measures, called mini-batch measures. We also define the class of multipattern risk-averse problems that generalizes the class of linear systems. We use both concepts in a feature-based $Q$-learning method with multipattern $Q$-factor approximation and we prove a high-probability regret bound of $\mathcal{O}\big(H^2 N^H \sqrt{ K}\big)$, where $H$ is the horizon, $N$ is the mini-batch size, and $K$ is the number of episodes. We also propose an economical version of the $Q$-learning method that streamlines the policy evaluation (backward) step. The theoretical results are illustrated on a stochastic assignment problem and a short-horizon multi-armed bandit problem.

AI.tldr: Introduces mini-batch Markov coherent risk measures and multipattern risk-averse problems for Q-learning with theoretical regret bounds and practical algorithmic improvements.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses risk-averse finite-horizon Markov Decision Problems, aiming to develop more efficient and theoretically-grounded methods for handling risk in reinforcement learning, particularly for problems with coherent risk measures.
AI.method: 1. Introduces mini-batch Markov coherent risk measures as a special class of risk measures. 2. Defines multipattern risk-averse problems generalizing linear systems. 3. Develops feature-based Q-learning with multipattern Q-factor approximation. 4. Proposes an economical version of Q-learning that streamlines the policy evaluation step.
AI.result: 1. Proves a high-probability regret bound of O(H² Nᴴ √K) where H is horizon, N is mini-batch size, and K is number of episodes. 2. Demonstrates theoretical results on stochastic assignment problem and short-horizon multi-armed bandit problem.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully combines mini-batch risk measures with multipattern risk-averse problems to develop efficient Q-learning algorithms with strong theoretical guarantees, validated on practical applications.

title: Group Cognition Learning: Making Everything Better Through Governed Two-Stage Agents Collaboration
comment: This study has been Accepted by ICML 2026. The current version is a manuscript, please refer to the official version released at ICML 2026 for the final published version
summary: Centralized multimodal learning commonly compresses language, acoustic, and visual signals into a single fused representation for prediction. While effective, this paradigm suffers from two limitations: modality dominance, where optimization gravitates towards the path of least resistance, ignoring weaker but informative modalities, and spurious modality coupling, where models overfit to incidental cross-modal correlations. To address these, we propose Group Cognition Learning (GCL), a governed collaboration paradigm that applies a two-stage protocol after modality-specific encoding. In Stage 1 (Selective Interaction), a Routing Agent proposes directed interaction routes, and an Auditing Agent assigns sample-wise gates to emphasize exchanges that yield positive marginal predictive gain while suppressing redundant coupling. In Stage 2 (Consensus Formation), a Public-Factor Agent maintains an explicit shared factor, and an Aggregation Agent produces the final prediction through contribution-aware weighting while keeping each modality representation as a specialization channel. Extensive experiments on CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, and MIntRec demonstrate that GCL mitigates dominance and coupling, establishing state-of-the-art results across both regression and classification benchmarks. Analysis experiments further demonstrate the effectiveness of the design.

AI.tldr: GCL addresses modality dominance and spurious coupling in multimodal learning via a two-stage governed collaboration paradigm, achieving SOTA results.
AI.motivation: Centralized multimodal learning suffers from modality dominance (optimization favors dominant modalities, ignoring weaker ones) and spurious modality coupling (models overfit to incidental cross-modal correlations).
AI.method: Proposes Group Cognition Learning (GCL) with two stages: Selective Interaction (Routing Agent proposes directed interaction routes, Auditing Agent assigns sample-wise gates) and Consensus Formation (Public-Factor Agent maintains explicit shared factor, Aggregation Agent produces final prediction with contribution-aware weighting).
AI.result: GCL establishes state-of-the-art results on CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, and MIntRec benchmarks for both regression and classification tasks, with analysis demonstrating design effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The paper introduces GCL as an effective solution to modality dominance and spurious coupling in centralized multimodal learning, achieving SOTA performance while offering interpretability.

title: Augmented Lagrangian Multiplier Network for State-wise Safety in Reinforcement Learning
summary: Safety is a primary challenge in real-world reinforcement learning (RL). Formulating safety requirements as state-wise constraints has become a prominent paradigm. Handling state-wise constraints with the Lagrangian method requires a distinct multiplier for every state, necessitating neural networks to approximate them as a multiplier network. However, applying standard dual gradient ascent to multiplier networks induces severe training oscillations. This is because the inherent instability of dual ascent is exacerbated by network generalization -- local overshoots and delayed updates propagate to adjacent states, further amplifying policy fluctuations. Existing stabilization techniques are designed for scalar multipliers, which are inadequate for state-dependent multiplier networks. To address this challenge, we propose an augmented Lagrangian multiplier network (ALaM) framework for stable learning of state-wise multipliers. ALaM consists of two key components. First, a quadratic penalty is introduced into the augmented Lagrangian to compensate for delayed multiplier updates and establish the local convexity near the optimum, thereby mitigating policy oscillations. Second, the multiplier network is trained via supervised regression toward a dual target, which stabilizes training and promotes convergence. Theoretically, we show that ALaM guarantees multiplier convergence and thus recovers the optimal policy of the constrained problem. Building on this framework, we integrate soft actor-critic (SAC) with ALaM to develop the SAC-ALaM algorithm. Experiments demonstrate that SAC-ALaM outperforms state-of-the-art safe RL baselines in both safety and return, while also stabilizing training dynamics and learning well-calibrated multipliers for risk identification.

AI.tldr: ALaM framework stabilizes state-wise constraint learning by combining quadratic penalty with supervised regression of multiplier networks, enabling better safe RL performance.
AI.motivation: Standard dual gradient ascent applied to state-dependent multiplier networks causes severe training oscillations due to instability exacerbated by network generalization, where local overshoots and delayed updates propagate to adjacent states.
AI.method: Two key components: 1) Introducing a quadratic penalty in the augmented Lagrangian to compensate for delayed multiplier updates and establish local convexity near optimum, and 2) Training multiplier network via supervised regression toward a dual target for stabilization.
AI.result: SAC-ALaM algorithm outperforms state-of-the-art safe RL baselines in both safety and return, stabilizes training dynamics, and learns well-calibrated multipliers for risk identification.
AI.conclusion: The ALaM framework with SAC-ALaM algorithm provides an effective solution to stabilize multiplier network training for state-wise safety constraints, outperforming existing safe RL baselines while ensuring safer training dynamics and better risk identification.

title: Advancing Edge Classification through High-Dimensional Causal Modeling of Node-Edge Interplay
summary: Edge classification, a crucial task for graph applications, remains relatively under-explored compared to link prediction. Current methods often overlook the potential causal influences of node features on edge features, leading to a loss of relevant prior information. In this work, we present an empirical exploration using the Causal Edge Classification Framework (CECF). Unlike conventional causal inference methods, CECF is the first framework to apply causal inference principles to the edge classification task and to explore modeling edge features as a high-dimensional treatment within a causal framework. Based on the node embedding of Graph Neural Network (GNN), CECF seeks to learn a balanced representation of high-dimensional edge features by mitigating the potential influence of node features. Then, a cross-attention network captures the complex dependencies between node and edge features for final edge classification.Extensive experiments demonstrate that CECF not only achieves superior performance but also serves as a flexible, plug-and-play enhancement for existing methods.We also provide empirical analyses, offering insights into when and how this high-dimensional causal modeling framework works for the edge classification.

AI.tldr: CECF is a novel causal inference framework for edge classification that treats edge features as high-dimensional treatments and balances them to mitigate node feature influence, achieving state-of-the-art performance as a plug-and-play enhancement.
AI.motivation: Edge classification is under-explored compared to link prediction, and current methods fail to account for causal influences of node features on edge features, losing relevant prior information.
AI.method: CECF uses node embeddings from GNNs, learns balanced representations of high-dimensional edge features by mitigating node feature influence, then employs a cross-attention network to capture complex node-edge dependencies for final classification.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show CECF achieves superior performance and serves as a flexible plug-and-play enhancement for existing methods, with empirical analyses providing insights into when and how the framework works.
AI.conclusion: CECF successfully applies causal inference principles to edge classification by treating edge features as high-dimensional treatments, establishing a new framework that both improves performance and provides interpretable insights into the task.

title: ResRL: Boosting LLM Reasoning via Negative Sample Projection Residual Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) enhances reasoning of Large Language Models (LLMs) but usually exhibits limited generation diversity due to the over-incentivization of positive rewards. Although methods like Negative Sample Reinforcement (NSR) mitigate this issue by upweighting penalty from negative samples, they may suppress the semantic distributions shared between positive and negative responses. To boost reasoning ability without losing diversity, this paper proposes negative sample projection Residual Reinforcement Learning (ResRL) that decouples similar semantic distributions among positive and negative responses. We theoretically link Lazy Likelihood Displacement (LLD) to negative-positive head-gradient interference and derive a single-forward proxy that upper-bounds representation alignment to guide conservative advantage reweighting. ResRL then projects negative-token hidden representations onto an SVD-based low-rank positive subspace and uses projection residuals to modulate negative gradients, improving reasoning while preserving diversity and outperforming strong baselines on average across twelve benchmarks spanning Mathematics, Code, Agent Tasks, and Function Calling. Notably, ResRL surpasses NSR on mathematical reasoning by 9.4\% in Avg@16 and 7.0\% in Pass@128. Code is available at https://github.com/1229095296/ResRL.git.

AI.tldr: ResRL (Residual Reinforcement Learning) addresses RLVR's limited diversity issue by projecting negative sample representations onto positive subspaces, using residuals to modulate negative gradients, improving reasoning while preserving diversity.
AI.motivation: RLVR enhances LLM reasoning but limits diversity due to over-incentivization of positive rewards. NSR mitigates this but suppresses shared semantic distributions between positive and negative responses.
AI.method: Negative sample projection ResRL decouples similar semantic distributions by projecting negative-token hidden representations onto SVD-based low-rank positive subspace and using projection residuals to modulate negative gradients.
AI.result: ResRL improves reasoning while preserving diversity, outperforming baselines on 12 benchmarks (Mathematics, Code, Agent Tasks, Function Calling). Notably beats NSR by 9.4% in Avg@16 and 7.0% in Pass@128 on math reasoning.
AI.conclusion: ResRL effectively addresses RLVR's diversity limitation by decoupling shared semantic distributions through negative sample projection, balancing reasoning improvement with generation diversity.

title: PILIR: Physics-Informed Local Implicit Representation
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks have become a powerful mesh-free method for solving partial differential equations, but their performance is often limited by spectral bias. Specifically, in standard MLPs used in PINNs, the global parameter coupling causes the model to prioritize learning low-frequency components, resulting in slow convergence for high-frequency details. To overcome this limitation, we introduce the Physics-Informed Local Implicit Representation (PILIR). Our approach separates the global physical domain into a discrete latent feature space and a continuous generative decoder. By using a learnable grid to encode explicit spatial locality, PILIR can capture high-frequency details locally, preventing dilution by global patterns. A generative neural operator then synthesizes these local latent features into continuous physical fields, allowing accurate reconstruction of fine-scale structures. Experiments on a range of challenging PDEs show that PILIR effectively mitigates spectral bias, thereby boosting the convergence of high-frequency details and achieving superior accuracy compared to state-of-the-art methods.

title: Towards Robust and Scalable Density-based Clustering via Graph Propagation
comment: arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2508.02989
summary: We present \textit{CluProp}, a novel framework that reimagines varied-density clustering in high-dimensional spaces as a label propagation process over neighborhood graphs. Our approach formally bridges the gap between density-based clustering and graph connectivity, leveraging efficient propagation mechanisms from network science to mitigate the parameter sensitivity inherent in traditional density-based methods. Specifically, we introduce a deterministic density-based propagation strategy to ensure scalable neighborhood identification.
