The framework is agnostic to the choice of distance metric and exhibits superior performance on large-scale data, processing millions of points in minutes while consistently outperforming existing baselines in accuracy.

AI.tldr: CluProp is a novel clustering framework that treats high-dimensional varied-density clustering as label propagation over neighborhood graphs, bridging density-based clustering with graph connectivity for scalable, metric-agnostic performance.
AI.motivation: Traditional density-based clustering methods suffer from parameter sensitivity, especially in high-dimensional spaces with varied densities. There's a need for more robust approaches that can handle large-scale data efficiently while maintaining accuracy.
AI.method: Reformulates varied-density clustering as a label propagation process over neighborhood graphs. Uses deterministic density-based propagation strategy for scalable neighborhood identification. The framework is metric-agnostic and leverages efficient propagation mechanisms from network science.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior performance on large-scale data, processing millions of points in minutes while consistently outperforming existing baselines in accuracy. The method is highly scalable and efficient for high-dimensional clustering tasks.
AI.conclusion: CluProp successfully bridges the gap between density-based clustering and graph connectivity, offering a parameter-robust, scalable solution for high-dimensional varied-density clustering that outperforms traditional approaches.

title: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning with Double Oracle Efficiency in Policy Optimization and Offline Estimation
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) in large environments often suffers from severe computational bottlenecks, as conventional regret minimization algorithms require repeated, costly calls to planning and statistical estimation oracles. While recent advances have explored offline oracle-efficient algorithms, their computational complexity typically scales with the cardinality of the state and action spaces, rendering them intractable for large-scale or continuous environments. In this paper, we address this fundamental limitation by studying offline oracle-efficient episodic RL through the lens of log-barrier and log-determinant regularization. Specifically, for tabular Markov Decision Processes (MDPs), we propose a novel algorithm that achieves the optimal $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T})$ regret bound while requiring only $O(H\log\log T)$ calls to both the offline statistical estimation and planning oracles when $T$ is known and $O(H\log T)$ calls when $T$ is unknown. Crucially, this oracle complexity is entirely independent of the size of the state and action spaces. This strict independence drastically reduces the planning oracle complexity, representing a substantial improvement over existing offline oracle-efficient algorithms (Qian et al., 2024). Furthermore, we demonstrate the versatility of our framework by generalizing the algorithm to linear MDPs featuring infinite state spaces and arbitrary action spaces. We prove that this generalized approach successfully attains meaningful sub-linear regret. Consequently, our work yields the first doubly oracle-efficient (i.e., efficient with respect to both statistical estimation and policy optimization) regret minimization algorithm capable of solving MDPs with infinite state and action spaces, significantly expanding the boundaries of computationally tractable RL.

AI.tldr: Novel log-barrier/log-determinant regularization algorithm achieves optimal regret with oracle calls independent of state/action space sizes, enabling doubly oracle-efficient RL even for infinite MDPs.
AI.motivation: Conventional RL algorithms suffer from computational bottlenecks in large environments, requiring repeated costly oracle calls that scale with state/action space sizes. Existing offline oracle-efficient algorithms still have complexity dependent on state/action cardinalities, making them intractable for large-scale or continuous environments.
AI.method: Uses log-barrier and log-determinant regularization for offline oracle-efficient episodic RL. For tabular MDPs, proposes algorithm requiring only O(H log log T) oracle calls when T known or O(H log T) when unknown. Generalizes framework to linear MDPs with infinite state spaces and arbitrary action spaces.
AI.result: Achieves optimal Õ(√T) regret with oracle complexity entirely independent of state/action space sizes. Reduces planning oracle complexity drastically compared to prior work. Successfully attains meaningful sub-linear regret for linear MDPs with infinite state spaces.
AI.conclusion: First doubly oracle-efficient regret minimization algorithm capable of solving MDPs with infinite state and action spaces, significantly expanding boundaries of computationally tractable RL by removing dependence on state/action space sizes in oracle complexity.

title: Mesh Field Theory: Port-Hamiltonian Formulation of Mesh-Based Physics
comment: 23 pages, 4 figures, 10 tables. Accepted to ICML 2026
summary: We present Mesh Field Theory (MeshFT) and its neural realization, MeshFT-Net: a structure-preserving framework for mesh-based continuum physics that cleanly separates the physics' topological structure from its metric structure. Imposing minimal physical principles (locality, permutation equivariance, orientation covariance, and energy balance/dissipation inequality), we prove a reduction theorem for mesh-based physics. Under these conditions, the physical dynamics admit a local factorization into a port-Hamiltonian form: the conservative interconnection is fixed uniquely by mesh topology, whereas metric effects enter only through constitutive relations and dissipation. This reduction clarifies what must be fixed and what should be learned, directly informing MeshFT-Net's design. Across evaluations on analytic and realistic datasets, physics-consistency tests, and out-of-distribution validation, MeshFT-Net achieves near-zero energy drift and strong physical fidelity (correct dispersion and momentum conservation) along with robust extrapolation and high data efficiency. By eliminating non-physical degrees of freedom and learning only metric-dependent structure, MeshFT provides a principled inductive bias for stable, faithful, and data-efficient learning-based physical simulation.

AI.tldr: Mesh Field Theory (MeshFT) is a framework separating mesh-based physics into topological structure and metric structure, leading to MeshFT-Net which achieves physically consistent simulations with near-zero energy drift and high data efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current mesh-based physics simulations often lack clear separation between topological and metric structure, leading to non-physical degrees of freedom and reduced data efficiency in learning-based approaches. The authors aim to create a principled framework that properly structures mesh-based continuum physics.
AI.method: The authors develop Mesh Field Theory (MeshFT) based on minimal physical principles (locality, permutation equivariance, orientation covariance, and energy balance/dissipation inequality). They prove a reduction theorem showing physical dynamics factor into port-Hamiltonian form where topological structure determines conservative interconnection and metric effects enter through constitutive relations. This informs MeshFT-Net design, which learns only metric-dependent structure while preserving physical topology.
AI.result: MeshFT-Net achieves near-zero energy drift and strong physical fidelity including correct dispersion and momentum conservation across analytic and realistic datasets. It shows robust extrapolation, high data efficiency, and passes physics-consistency tests with strong out-of-distribution validation performance.
AI.conclusion: By cleanly separating topological from metric structure and learning only the latter, MeshFT provides a principled inductive bias for stable, faithful, and data-efficient learning-based physical simulation, eliminating non-physical degrees of freedom while maintaining physical consistency.

title: M-CaStLe: Uncovering Local Causal Structures in Multivariate Space-Time Gridded Data
comment: 19 pages and 6 figures in the main text; 33 pages and 11 figures total
summary:
Causal graph discovery for space-time systems is challenging in high-dimensional gridded data, which often has many more grid cells than temporal observations per cell. The Causal Space-Time Stencil Learning (CaStLe) meta-algorithm was developed to address that niche under space-time locality and stationarity assumptions, but it is currently limited to univariate analyses. In this work, we present M-CaStLe. M-CaStLe generalizes the local embedding and parent-identification phases of CaStLe to jointly model local within-variable and cross-variable space-time causal structures in gridded data. Like CaStLe, by constraining candidate parents to a constant-size space-time neighborhood and pooling spatial replicates, M-CaStLe increases effective sample size to make discovery tractable in high-dimensional settings. We further decompose the resulting multivariate stencil graph into reaction and spatial graphs to aid interpretation in complex settings.
  We study M-CaStLe in four settings: a multivariate space-time vector autoregression benchmark with known ground truth, an advective-diffusive-reaction partial differential equation verification problem with derived physical reference structure, an atmospheric chemistry case study in a low-temporal-sample regime, and an El Niño Southern Oscillation study on reanalysis data, identifying phase-dependent ocean--atmosphere coupling. Across these settings, M-CaStLe more accurately recovers multivariate causal structure in controlled settings and identifies important physical dynamics in real-world case studies. Overall, M-CaStLe advances causal discovery for multivariate space-time systems while retaining interpretability at the grid level.

AI.tldr: M-CaStLe extends CaStLe to multivariate space-time causal discovery, jointly modeling local within-variable and cross-variable dependencies, improving accuracy in controlled and real-world settings.
AI.motivation: Causal graph discovery for space-time systems is challenging in high-dimensional gridded data with many more grid cells than temporal observations. The existing CaStLe meta-algorithm addresses this but is limited to univariate analyses.
AI.method: M-CaStLe generalizes CaStLe to jointly model local within-variable and cross-variable space-time causal structures. It constrains candidate parents to a constant-size space-time neighborhood and pools spatial replicates to increase effective sample size. The multivariate stencil graph is decomposed into reaction and spatial graphs for better interpretation.
AI.result: M-CaStLe more accurately recovers multivariate causal structure in controlled settings (VAR benchmark and PDE verification) and identifies important physical dynamics in real-world case studies (atmospheric chemistry and El Niño Southern Oscillation).
AI.conclusion: Overall, M-CaStLe advances causal discovery for multivariate space-time systems while retaining interpretability at the grid level.

title: Rethinking LLM Ensembling from the Perspective of Mixture Models
summary: Model ensembling is a well-established technique for improving the performance of machine learning models. Conventionally, this involves averaging the output distributions of multiple models and selecting the most probable label. This idea has been naturally extended to large language models (LLMs), yielding improved performance but incurring substantial computational cost. This inefficiency stems from directly applying conventional ensemble implementation to LLMs, which require a separate forward pass for each model to explicitly compute the ensemble distribution. In this paper, we propose the Mixture-model-like Ensemble (ME). By reinterpreting the ensemble as a mixture model, ME stochastically selects a single model at each step to generate the next token, thereby avoiding the need to explicitly compute the full ensemble distribution. ME is mathematically equivalent to sampling from the ensemble distribution, but requires invoking only one model, making it 1.78x-2.68x faster than conventional ensemble. Furthermore, this perspective connects LLM ensembling and token-level routing methods, suggesting that LLM ensembling is a special case of routing methods. Our findings open new avenues for efficient LLM ensembling and motivate further exploration of token-level routing strategies for LLMs. Our code is available at https://github.com/jialefu/Mixture-model-like-Ensemble/.

AI.tldr: Proposes Mixture-model-like Ensemble (ME) for efficient LLM ensembling by stochastically selecting a single model per token step, achieving 1.78x-2.68x speedup and linking ensembling to token-level routing methods.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the computational inefficiency of conventional model ensembling applied to large language models (LLMs). Traditional ensemble methods require separate forward passes for each model to compute the ensemble distribution, leading to substantial computational costs. The goal is to develop a more efficient ensemble technique that maintains performance while reducing computational overhead.
AI.method: The authors propose the Mixture-model-like Ensemble (ME), which reinterprets the ensemble as a mixture model. Instead of averaging output distributions from multiple models, ME stochastically selects a single model at each token generation step to produce the next token, thereby avoiding explicit computation of the full ensemble distribution.
AI.result: ME is mathematically equivalent to sampling from the ensemble distribution but requires invoking only one model per step, resulting in a speedup of 1.78x to 2.68x compared to conventional ensemble methods. Additionally, the paper shows that LLM ensembling can be viewed as a special case of token-level routing methods, providing a new perspective on the relationship between these techniques.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that ME offers a novel and efficient approach to LLM ensembling by reinterpreting it as a mixture model, which not only provides significant speed improvements but also establishes a theoretical connection to token-level routing methods, thereby opening new research directions for efficient ensemble strategies in large language models.

title: GD4: Graph-based Discrete Denoising Diffusion for MIMO Detection
summary: In wireless communications, recovering the optimal solution to the multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) detection problem is NP-hard. Obtaining high-quality suboptimal solutions with a favorable performance-complexity trade-off is particularly challenging in under-determined systems with $N_t$ transmit antennas and $N_r < N_t$ receive antennas. Recent diffusion-based MIMO detectors have shown promise, but they require extensive sampling iterations at inference time, and their performance degrades in under-determined scenarios. We propose GD4, a graph-based discrete denoising diffusion method for MIMO detection. Unlike existing diffusion-based detectors that operate in a continuous relaxed space, GD4 performs denoising directly in the discrete symbol space and enables fast inference with one or a few denoising evaluations. Numerical results show that, under a similar inference-time compute budget, GD4 produces higher-quality suboptimal solutions than existing diffusion-based detectors and some widely used classical baseline including box-constrained Babai point and the $K$-best box-constrained randomized Klein-Babai point in both under-determined and overdetermined settings.

AI.tldr: GD4: A graph-based discrete denoising diffusion method for MIMO detection that operates directly in discrete symbol space for fast inference with one or few denoising evaluations, outperforming existing diffusion-based detectors and classical baselines.
AI.motivation: MIMO detection is NP-hard, especially challenging in under-determined systems (N_r < N_t). Existing diffusion-based detectors require extensive sampling iterations and suffer performance degradation in under-determined scenarios.
AI.method: Proposed GD4 (graph-based discrete denoising diffusion) performs denoising directly in discrete symbol space rather than continuous relaxed space, enabling fast inference with minimal denoising evaluations.
AI.result: Under similar inference-time compute budget, GD4 produces higher-quality suboptimal solutions than existing diffusion-based detectors and classical baselines (box-constrained Babai point and K-best box-constrained randomized Klein-Babai point) in both under-determined and overdetermined settings.
AI.conclusion: GD4 provides a favorable performance-complexity trade-off for MIMO detection by enabling fast inference through discrete space denoising while maintaining superior solution quality across various system configurations.

title: Optimal Spatio-Temporal Decoupling for Bayesian Conformal Prediction
summary: Online Conformal Prediction (CP) struggles to balance temporal adaptability and structural stability. Feedback-driven methods (e.g., Adaptive Conformal Inference (ACI)) suffer from systemic marginal under-coverage and high interval variance during abrupt shifts, while temporally discounted Bayesian CP suffers from severe structural lag and uncalibrated interval bloat. We propose State-Adaptive Bayesian Conformal Prediction (SA-BCP) to achieve optimal spatio-temporal decoupling. By gating long-term temporal inertia with spatial kernel-density evidence, SA-BCP proactively expands intervals for recognized historical regimes while maintaining tight efficiency during stable states. We rigorously prove this mechanism's optimality, identifying a minimax bias-variance tradeoff governed by an evidence threshold $K$. Extensive benchmarks on volatile financial datasets (2016--2026), including AMD, Gold, and GBP/USD, demonstrate that SA-BCP consistently minimizes the strictly proper Winkler score across diverse confidence levels. Specifically, SA-BCP resolves the systematic under-coverage inherent to ACI variants while simultaneously reducing the uncalibrated interval bloat of Bayesian CP by 10\% to 37\% under high-confidence requests. By elegantly navigating this tradeoff, SA-BCP achieves an optimal balance between conditional reliability and predictive efficiency.

AI.tldr: SA-BCP achieves optimal balance between temporal adaptability and structural stability in online conformal prediction by gating long-term temporal inertia with spatial evidence, resolving systematic under-coverage of feedback methods while reducing interval bloat of Bayesian approaches.
AI.motivation: Online Conformal Prediction struggles with balancing temporal adaptability and structural stability. Feedback-driven methods like Adaptive Conformal Inference suffer from systemic marginal under-coverage and high interval variance during abrupt shifts, while temporally discounted Bayesian CP suffers from structural lag and uncalibrated interval bloat.
AI.method: Propose State-Adaptive Bayesian Conformal Prediction (SA-BCP) for optimal spatio-temporal decoupling. It gates long-term temporal inertia with spatial kernel-density evidence, proactively expanding intervals for recognized historical regimes while maintaining tight efficiency during stable states. The mechanism's optimality is rigorously proved with a minimax bias-variance tradeoff governed by an evidence threshold K.
AI.result: Extensive benchmarks on volatile financial datasets (2016-2026) including AMD, Gold, and GBP/USD demonstrate SA-BCP consistently minimizes the strictly proper Winkler score across diverse confidence levels. It resolves systematic under-coverage inherent to ACI variants while reducing uncalibrated interval bloat of Bayesian CP by 10% to 37% under high-confidence requests.
AI.conclusion: SA-BCP elegantly navigates the tradeoff between conditional reliability and predictive efficiency, achieving an optimal balance by proactively adapting to recognized regimes while maintaining efficiency during stable states.

title: Adaptive Equilibrium: Dynamic Weighting Framework for Generalized Interruption of DeepFake Models
summary: The advancement of generalized deepfake disruption is constrained by the interruption imbalance, a fundamental bottleneck inherent to the generation of universal perturbations. We reveal that conventional static gradient normalization fundamentally struggles to resolve architectural conflicts, causing the optimization to bias towards susceptible models while neglecting resistant ones. We argue that achieving high and uniform effectiveness requires resolving this imbalance by reaching an adaptive equilibrium. We propose the Adaptive Equilibrium Framework (AEF), which employs a dynamic weighting mechanism that utilizes real-time loss feedback to adaptively assign greater interruption weights to the most resistant models. This approach shifts the optimization from an average-case problem to finding a dynamic balance, driving the perturbation to a uniformly effective equilibrium state. Comprehensive experiments validate that AEF achieves a more balanced interruption performance, maintaining a consistent interruption success rate across the evaluated diverse architectures.

AI.tldr: Addresses interruption imbalance in deepfake disruption via Adaptive Equilibrium Framework (AEF) using dynamic weighting based on real-time loss feedback to achieve uniform effectiveness across diverse architectures.
AI.motivation: The advancement of generalized deepfake disruption is constrained by interruption imbalance, a bottleneck in universal perturbation generation. Conventional static gradient normalization struggles to resolve architectural conflicts, causing optimization bias toward susceptible models while neglecting resistant ones.
AI.method: The paper proposes the Adaptive Equilibrium Framework (AEF) which employs a dynamic weighting mechanism that utilizes real-time loss feedback to adaptively assign greater interruption weights to the most resistant models, shifting optimization from an average-case problem to finding a dynamic balance.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments validate that AEF achieves more balanced interruption performance, maintaining consistent interruption success rates across diverse architectures compared to conventional methods.
AI.conclusion: The paper introduces the Adaptive Equilibrium Framework (AEF) as an effective solution to address interruption imbalance in generalized deepfake disruption, achieving more balanced and uniformly effective interruption performance across diverse deepfake architectures.

title: The Power of Order: Fooling LLMs with Adversarial Table Permutations
summary: Large Language Models have achieved remarkable success and are increasingly deployed in critical applications involving tabular data, such as Table Question Answering. However, their robustness to the structure of this input remains a critical, unaddressed question. This paper demonstrates that modern LLMs exhibit a significant vulnerability to the layout of tabular data. Specifically, we show that semantically-invariant permutations of rows and columns - rearrangements that do not alter the table's underlying information - are sometimes sufficient to cause incorrect or inconsistent model outputs. To systematically probe this vulnerability, we introduce Adversarial Table Permutation, a novel, gradient-based attack that efficiently identifies worst-case permutations designed to maximally disrupt model performance. Our extensive experiments demonstrate that ATP significantly degrades the performance of a wide range of LLMs. This reveals a pervasive vulnerability across different model sizes and architectures, including the most recent and popular models. Our findings expose a fundamental weakness in how current LLMs process structured data, underscoring the urgent need to develop permutation-robust models for reliable, real-world applications.

AI.tldr: LLMs are vulnerable to semantically-invariant table layout permutations; ATP attack efficiently finds worst-case permutations to degrade performance.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed in critical applications involving tabular data, but their robustness to the structure of this input remains unaddressed.
AI.method: Introduce Adversarial Table Permutation (ATP), a novel gradient-based attack that efficiently identifies worst-case permutations designed to maximally disrupt model performance.
AI.result: ATP significantly degrades the performance of a wide range of LLMs, revealing a pervasive vulnerability across different model sizes and architectures.
AI.conclusion: The paper reveals a pervasive vulnerability in LLMs' processing of structured data, highlighting the urgent need to develop permutation-robust models for reliable real-world applications.

title: Federated Learning with Hypergradient-based Online Update of Aggregation Weights
summary: Federated learning using mobile and Internet of Things devices requires not only the ability to handle heterogeneity of clients' data distributions but also high adaptability to varying communication environments. We propose FedHAW (Federated Learning with Hypergradient-based update of Aggregation Weights) that implements online updates of aggregation weights. FedHAW updates the aggregation weights by using hypergradient, the gradient of the objective function with respect to the weights, which can be calculated with low computational overhead. Simulation results show that the proposed method possesses high generalization performance in heterogeneous environments and high robustness to communication errors.

AI.tldr: FedHAW: A federated learning method that dynamically adjusts aggregation weights using hypergradients to improve generalization and robustness in heterogeneous mobile/IoT environments with varying communication conditions.
AI.motivation: Federated learning in mobile and IoT environments faces two key challenges: 1) heterogeneity of clients' data distributions across different devices, and 2) high variability in communication environments that can cause errors and disruptions.
AI.method: The proposed FedHAW method implements online updates of aggregation weights using hypergradients - the gradient of the objective function with respect to the aggregation weights. This allows dynamic adjustment of how much weight to give each client's model updates during aggregation, with low computational overhead.
AI.result: Simulation results demonstrate that FedHAW achieves high generalization performance in heterogeneous environments and shows high robustness to communication errors compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: FedHAW provides an effective solution for federated learning in challenging mobile and IoT settings by dynamically optimizing aggregation weights through hypergradient-based updates, balancing performance across heterogeneous data distributions while maintaining robustness to communication issues.

title: Batch Normalization for Neural Networks on Complex Domains
summary: Riemannian neural networks have proven effective in solving a variety of machine learning tasks. The key to their success lies in the development of principled Riemannian analogs of fundamental building blocks in deep neural networks (DNNs). Among those, Riemannian batch normalization (BN) layers have shown to enhance training stability and improve accuracy. In this paper, we propose BN layers for neural networks on complex domains. The proposed layers have close connections with existing Riemannian BN layers. We derive essential components for practical implementations of BN layers on some complex domains which are less studied in previous works, e.g., the Siegel disk domain. We conduct experiments on radar clutter classification, node classification, and action recognition demonstrating the efficacy of our method.

AI.tldr: Introduces BN layers for neural networks on complex domains, deriving practical implementations for less studied domains like Siegel disk.
AI.motivation: Riemannian batch normalization has proven effective for enhancing training stability and accuracy. However, existing work focuses primarily on real-valued Riemannian spaces, while complex domains are less explored. This paper aims to extend batch normalization to complex domains.
AI.method: The authors derive essential components for practical implementations of batch normalization layers on complex domains, with a focus on less studied domains such as the Siegel disk domain.
AI.result: Experiments on radar clutter classification, node classification, and action recognition demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed method.
AI.conclusion: A complex domain version of batch normalization is developed, offering improved training stability and accuracy for neural networks on complex domains like the Siegel disk. The experimental results across multiple tasks show the efficacy of the method.

title: Near-optimal and Efficient First-Order Algorithm for Multi-Task Learning with Shared Linear Representation
summary: Multi-task learning (MTL) has emerged as a pivotal paradigm in machine learning by leveraging shared structures across multiple related tasks. Despite its empirical success, the development of likelihood-based efficiently solvable algorithms--even for shared linear representations--remains largely underdeveloped, primarily due to the non-convex structure intrinsic to matrix factorization. This paper introduces a first-order algorithm that jointly learns a shared representation and task-specific parameters, with guaranteed efficiency. Notably, it converges in $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(1)$ iterations and attains a \emph{near-optimal} estimation error of $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(dk/(TN))$, \emph{improving} over existing likelihood-based methods by a factor of $k$, where $d$, $k$, $T$, $N$ denote input dimension, representation dimension, task count, and samples per task, respectively. Our results justify that likelihood-based first-order methods can efficiently solve the MTL problem.

AI.tldr: A first-order algorithm for multi-task learning achieves efficient convergence and near-optimal estimation error, improving over existing likelihood-based methods by factor k.
AI.motivation: MTL leverages shared structures across tasks but lacks efficient solvable likelihood-based algorithms due to non-convex matrix factorization, needing improvement over existing methods.
AI.method: Developed a first-order algorithm that jointly learns shared representation and task-specific parameters with guaranteed efficiency.
AI.result: Algorithm converges in Õ(1) iterations with near-optimal estimation error Õ(dk/(TN)), improving by factor k over existing likelihood-based methods.
AI.conclusion: Likelihood-based first-order methods can efficiently solve MTL problem, providing justification for their use and demonstrating significant improvement over prior approaches.

title: Trading off rewards and errors in multi-armed bandits
comment: Published at AISTATS 2017 (20th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics)
summary: In multi-armed bandits, the most-explored arms are the most informative, while reward maximization typically pulls only the best arm. We study the tradeoff between identifying arm means accurately and accumulating reward, and present an algorithm with regret guarantees that interpolates between the two objectives. We provide both upper and lower bounds and validate empirically.

AI.tldr: An algorithm for multi-armed bandits balances exploration (accurate arm identification) and exploitation (reward maximization), with theoretical regret bounds and empirical validation.
AI.motivation: In multi-armed bandits, exploration (identifying arm means accurately) and exploitation (maximizing reward) are conflicting objectives. The motivation is to study and address the tradeoff between these two goals, as standard approaches often prioritize one over the other, leading to suboptimal performance in scenarios requiring both accurate identification and high cumulative reward.
AI.method: The authors propose a novel algorithm for the multi-armed bandit problem that balances exploration (identifying arm means accurately) and exploitation (accumulating reward). The method includes theoretical analysis providing upper and lower bounds on regret, and empirical validation to demonstrate performance.
AI.result: The paper presents an algorithm with regret guarantees that interpolates between exploration and exploitation objectives. Both upper and lower bounds on regret are provided, and empirical validation shows the algorithm's effectiveness in balancing the tradeoff.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that their algorithm effectively navigates the tradeoff between exploration and exploitation in multi-armed bandits, offering a flexible solution that can be tuned based on the desired balance between accurate arm identification and reward accumulation, supported by theoretical and empirical evidence.

title: Revealing graph bandits for maximizing local influence
comment: Published at AISTATS 2016 (19th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics)
summary: We study a graph bandit setting where the objective of the learner is to detect the most influential node of a graph by requesting as little information from the graph as possible. One of the relevant applications for this setting is marketing in social networks, where the marketer aims at finding and taking advantage of the most influential customers. The existing approaches for bandit problems on graphs require either partial or complete knowledge of the graph. In this paper, we do not assume any knowledge of the graph, but we consider a setting where it can be gradually discovered in a sequential and active way. At each round, the learner chooses a node of the graph and the only information it receives is a stochastic set of the nodes that the chosen node is currently influencing. To address this setting, we propose BARE, a bandit strategy for which we prove a regret guarantee that scales with the detectable dimension, a problem dependent quantity that is often much smaller than the number of nodes.

AI.tldr: Bandit strategy for finding the most influential node in a graph without any prior knowledge, using sequential active discovery and receiving stochastic influence information
AI.motivation: Marketing in social networks where marketers need to find influential customers; existing approaches require graph knowledge which is often unavailable
AI.method: BARE bandit strategy: at each round, learner chooses a node and receives stochastic set of influenced nodes; gradually discovers graph sequentially
AI.result: Proved regret guarantee scaling with detectable dimension, which is often much smaller than number of nodes
AI.conclusion: Provides efficient solution for graph bandit problems without prior graph knowledge, with improved regret bounds using problem-dependent detectable dimension

title: Distance metric learning for conditional anomaly detection
comment: Published at FLAIRS 2008 (21st International Florida AI Research Society Conference)
summary: Anomaly detection methods can be very useful in identifying unusual or interesting patterns in data. A recently proposed conditional anomaly detection framework extends anomaly detection to the problem of identifying anomalous patterns on a subset of attributes in the data. The anomaly always depends (is conditioned) on the value of remaining attributes. The work presented in this paper focuses on instance-based methods for detecting conditional anomalies. The methods depend heavily on the distance metric that lets us identify examples in the dataset that are most critical for detecting the anomaly. To optimize the performance of such methods we study and devise a metric learning method that learns the distance metric to reflect best the conditional anomaly pattern.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a metric learning approach for optimizing conditional anomaly detection methods, where anomalies depend on specific attribute values.
AI.motivation: Existing conditional anomaly detection methods rely heavily on distance metrics, and their performance can be optimized by learning better metrics that accurately reflect conditional anomaly patterns.
AI.method: The paper focuses on instance-based methods for conditional anomaly detection and develops a metric learning method that learns the optimal distance metric to identify anomalies conditioned on specific attribute values.
AI.result: The proposed metric learning method improves the performance of instance-based conditional anomaly detection by optimizing the distance metric to better capture conditional anomaly patterns in the data.
AI.conclusion: Learning appropriate distance metrics is crucial for effective conditional anomaly detection, and the proposed metric learning approach enhances the ability to identify anomalies that depend on specific contextual attributes.

title: Scaling Federated Linear Contextual Bandits via Sketching
summary: In federated contextual linear bandits, high data dimensionality incurs prohibitive computation and communication costs: local agents perform $O(d^3)$-time determinant computation and upload $O(d^2)$ parameters, making existing algorithms unscalable, where $d$ is the dimension of data. To relieve these scaling bottlenecks, this paper proposes Federated Sketch Contextual Linear Bandits (FSCLB). On the computation side, FSCLB uses SVD to indirectly obtain the determinant required for communication, eliminating the prohibitive cost of direct determinant calculation and cutting complexity from $O(d^3)$ to $O(l^2d)$ per round, where $l< d$ is the sketch size. On the communication side, FSCLB introduces a double-sketch strategy that reduces both upload and download costs from $O(d^2)$ to $O(ld)$. Naively involving sketch update into federated contextual linear bandits can destroy the local increment and invalidate the asynchronous communication condition; FSCLB solves this by replacing the covariance matrix with the sketch matrix when deciding whether to communicate. Theoretically, FSCLB achieves a regret bound of $\widetilde{O} ((\sqrt{d}+\sqrt{M\varepsilon_l})\sqrt{lT})$, where $\varepsilon_l$ is the upper bounded by the spectral tail of the covariance matrix; when $l$ exceeds the rank of the covariance matrix, the bound simplifies to $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{ldT})$, matching the optimal no-sketch regret. Experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets show that FSCLB significantly reduces computational and communication costs by over 90 \% while sacrificing only a negligible amount of cumulative reward.

AI.tldr: FSCLB is a federated sketch-based algorithm that dramatically reduces computation (from O(d³) to O(l²d)) and communication (from O(d²) to O(ld)) costs in federated contextual linear bandits while maintaining near-optimal regret bounds.
AI.motivation: Federated contextual linear bandits face severe scalability issues due to high-dimensional data - local agents need O(d³) time for determinant computation and upload O(d²) parameters, making existing algorithms impractical for large-scale applications.
AI.method: Proposes Federated Sketch Contextual Linear Bandits (FSCLB) with: 1) Using SVD instead of direct determinant calculation to reduce computation, 2) A double-sketch strategy to reduce both upload and download costs, 3) Replacing covariance matrix with sketch matrix for communication decisions to handle incremental updates while maintaining asynchronous communication.
AI.result: FSCLB achieves regret bound of Õ((√d+√Mεₗ)√lT), which simplifies to Õ(√ldT) when sketch size l exceeds covariance matrix rank. Experiments show over 90% reduction in computational and communication costs with only negligible loss in cumulative reward.
AI.conclusion: FSCLB effectively solves the scalability bottleneck in federated contextual linear bandits through sketching techniques, making the approach practical for real-world applications while maintaining theoretical guarantees and empirical performance.

title: LambdaRankIC: Directly Optimizing Rank IC for Financial Prediction
summary: In financial predictions, the performance of machine learning models is often assessed by Rank IC, which is the Spearman rank correlation between the model predictions and the realized asset returns. Despite its wide adoption, most existing models are trained using regression losses or ranking objectives that may not align with Rank IC. We propose LambdaRankIC, a novel learning-to-rank approach that directly optimizes Rank IC. We circumvent the non-differentiability of the ranking operator by deriving the closed-form expression for the lambda gradients induced by the pairwise rank swaps, which enables efficient gradient-based optimization within the LambdaRank framework. We implement LambdaRankIC as a custom objective in XGBoost. Theoretically, we show that our approach optimizes an upper bound on Rank IC. We evaluate the proposed approach on both simulated and real-world financial data. In simulation studies, LambdaRankIC accurately recovers the true ranking structure in noiseless settings and consistently outperforms regression-based and NDCG-oriented ranking methods under low signal-to-noise ratios and heavy-tailed noise regimes. In empirical experiments using real market data, LambdaRankIC achieves the best out-of-sample performance on evaluation metrics commonly used in finance, including Rank IC, ICIR, monthly return, and Sharpe ratio. These results show that directly optimizing Rank IC can yield substantial improvements over conventional learning objectives in financial predictions when the full-order ranking quality is the primary goal.

AI.tldr: A novel LambdaRankIC method that directly optimizes Rank IC (Spearman correlation) for financial predictions, outperforming regression and traditional ranking approaches by using pairwise swap gradients within XGBoost.
AI.motivation: Existing machine learning models for financial predictions use regression losses or ranking objectives that don't align well with Rank IC (Spearman correlation), which is the standard performance metric in finance. There's a misalignment between training objectives and evaluation metrics.
AI.method: LambdaRankIC - a learning-to-rank approach that directly optimizes Rank IC by deriving closed-form lambda gradients for pairwise rank swaps. The method circumvents non-differentiability of ranking operators by computing these gradients analytically. Implemented as a custom objective in XGBoost with theoretical guarantee of optimizing an upper bound on Rank IC.
AI.result: Simulation: Accurately recovers true ranking in noiseless settings and outperforms regression-based and NDCG-oriented methods under low SNR and heavy-tailed noise. Real-world: Achieves best out-of-sample performance on finance metrics including Rank IC, ICIR, monthly return, and Sharpe ratio.
AI.conclusion: Directly optimizing Rank IC yields substantial improvements over conventional learning objectives in financial predictions when full-order ranking quality is the primary goal, demonstrating the importance of aligning training objectives with evaluation metrics.

title: A Comparative Study of QSPR Methods on a Unique Multitask PAMPA dataset
summary:
We present a unique, multitask dataset comprising 143 drug and drug candidate molecules, each evaluated on in vitro, parallel artificial-membrane permeability assays (PAMPA) using six different model membranes. Using this resource, we systematically assess the effectiveness of various molecular descriptors and regression models in predicting passive membrane permeability. The studied models range from simple linear regression to a modern pre-trained transformer architecture. Particular attention is given to the trade-off between predictive performance and model interpretability, highlighting the challenges introduced by machine learning approaches. To our knowledge, this is the most comprehensive study on simultaneous modeling of multiple organ-specific PAMPA membranes to date, offering novel insights into membrane-specific permeability profiles.
  We found that expert-designed physico-chemical property descriptors are more fitting for a limited sample size permeabilty study than deep learning based representations.

AI.tldr: Study shows traditional physico-chemical descriptors beat deep learning for predicting drug permeability across multiple membrane types when data is limited.
AI.motivation: To conduct the most comprehensive study on simultaneous modeling of multiple organ-specific PAMPA membranes and understand the trade-off between predictive performance and model interpretability in passive membrane permeability prediction.
AI.method: Systematic assessment of various molecular descriptors and regression models (from simple linear regression to pre-trained transformer architectures) using a unique multitask dataset of 143 drug/drug candidate molecules evaluated on six different model membranes via PAMPA assays.
AI.result: Found that expert-designed physico-chemical property descriptors outperform deep learning representations for limited sample size permeability studies.
AI.conclusion: Expert-designed physico-chemical property descriptors are more suitable for limited sample size permeability studies than deep learning representations, highlighting the importance of balancing predictive performance with model interpretability.

title: Scale-Aware Adversarial Analysis: A Diagnostic for Generative AI in Multiscale Complex Systems
summary: Complex physical systems, from supersonic turbulence to the macroscopic structure of the universe, are governed by continuous multiscale dynamics. While modern machine learning architectures excel at mapping the high-dimensional observables of these systems, it remains unclear whether they internalize the governing physical laws or merely interpolate discrete statistical correlations. Standard Explainable AI (XAI) architectures, particularly perturbation-based and gradient-saliency methods, rely on pixel-wise perturbations, which generate unphysical artifacts and push inputs off the valid empirical distribution. To resolve this, we introduce a diagnostic framework driven by Constrained Diffusion Decomposition (CDD), a diffusion-based multiscale data decomposition algorithm that enables physically constrained data generation and model evaluation via scale-aware modifications. Applying this framework to a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM), we execute deterministic interventions directly within the continuous, CDD-based scale space. We demonstrate that under moderate physical perturbations, the unconstrained generative model exhibits localized structural freezing and non-linear instability rather than continuous PDE-like responses. The network fails to maintain cross-scale continuity, causing the generative trajectory to diverge when pushed into unseen physical states. By synthesizing a continuum of physically coherent states, this scale-informed methodology establishes a controlled test ground to evaluate algorithmic vulnerabilities, providing the rigorous physical constraints necessary for future architectures to respect the multiscale causality of the natural universe.

AI.tldr: CDD framework enables physically constrained scale-space interventions on diffusion models, revealing their inability to maintain cross-scale continuity when perturbed, highlighting need for physics-respecting architectures.
AI.motivation: Current ML architectures may not truly internalize physical laws governing continuous multiscale systems, and standard XAI methods using pixel-wise perturbations create unphysical artifacts that push inputs off valid distributions, limiting reliable evaluation.
AI.method: Introduces Constrained Diffusion Decomposition (CDD), a diffusion-based multiscale data decomposition algorithm for physically constrained data generation. Uses CDD to execute deterministic interventions in continuous scale space on DDPMs, analyzing model responses to physical perturbations.
AI.result: Unconstrained generative models exhibit localized structural freezing and non-linear instability rather than continuous PDE-like responses under moderate physical perturbations. Networks fail to maintain cross-scale continuity, causing generative trajectories to diverge when pushed into unseen physical states.
AI.conclusion: The scale-informed CDD framework establishes a controlled test environment to evaluate algorithmic vulnerabilities, providing the physical constraints necessary for future architectures to properly respect multiscale causality in physical systems.

title: Stable-GFlowNet: Toward Diverse and Robust LLM Red-Teaming via Contrastive Trajectory Balance
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) Red-Teaming, which proactively identifies vulnerabilities of LLMs, is an essential process for ensuring safety. Finding effective and diverse attacks in red-teaming is important, but achieving both is challenging. Generative Flow Networks (GFNs) that perform distribution matching are a promising methods, but they are notorious for training instability and mode collapse. In particular, unstable rewards in red-teaming accelerate mode collapse. We propose Stable-GFN (S-GFN), which eliminates partition function $Z$ estimation in GFN and reduces training instability. S-GFN avoids Z-estimation through pairwise comparisons and employs a robust masking methodology against noisy rewards. Additionally, we propose a fluency stabilizer to prevent the model from getting stuck in local optima that produce gibberish. S-GFN provides more stable training while maintaining the optimal policy of GFN. We demonstrate the overwhelming attack performance and diversity of S-GFN across various settings.

AI.tldr: A novel Stable-GFN (S-GFN) method is proposed to improve LLM red-teaming by eliminating partition function estimation in Generative Flow Networks, providing more stable training while maintaining attack performance and diversity.
AI.motivation: LLM red-teaming is essential for safety but faces challenges in finding both effective and diverse attacks. Generative Flow Networks (GFNs) are promising but suffer from training instability and mode collapse, especially with unstable rewards in red-teaming.
AI.method: Stable-GFN eliminates partition function Z estimation in GFN using pairwise comparisons and employs robust masking against noisy rewards. Also introduces a fluency stabilizer to prevent getting stuck in local optima that produce gibberish.
AI.result: S-GFN demonstrates overwhelming attack performance and diversity across various settings while providing more stable training while maintaining the optimal policy of GFN.
AI.conclusion: S-GFN successfully addresses the training instability and mode collapse issues in GFN-based red-teaming, offering a stable yet effective approach for finding diverse attacks in LLM safety testing.

title: Affinity Is Not Enough: Recovering the Free Energy Principle in Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Sparse MoE routing fails at domain transitions, where the current token belongs to one distribution and the next to another. In a controlled experiment (4 experts, 5 seeds), standard affinity routing assigns only 0.006 +/- 0.001 probability to the correct expert at the transition. Three lightweight gate modifications raise this to 0.748 +/- 0.002 (124x), cutting experts needed for 99% coverage from infeasible to a small constant: temporal memory (beta), a per-expert LIF membrane potential accumulating routing context across tokens; precision-weighted gating (Pi), a per-expert inverse variance of recent prediction error, yielding 31x contrast between reliable and unreliable experts; and anticipatory routing, a next-state predictor conditioned on the beta-accumulated hidden state. The mechanisms draw from Friston's Free Energy Principle and use LIF dynamics from spiking neural networks. An ablation across all 2^3 subsets reveals a super-additive beta x Ant interaction: anticipation alone gives nothing (+0.000 +/- 0.001); beta alone gives modest gain (+0.295 +/- 0.013); combined they close 75% of the oracle gap (+0.741 +/- 0.002, exceeding the sum by +0.446 +/- 0.014). This is structural: a stateless predictor cannot detect approaching transitions because pre-transition tokens are distributionally identical to within-domain tokens. In a character-level MoE LM (5 seeds), beta-routing reduces transition-step BPC from 6.56 +/- 0.01 (Standard) to 4.01 +/- 0.15 (beta-MoE); the beta + Ant gate places 0.86 +/- 0.02 probability on the correct domain expert before that domain appears in input, vs 0.42 +/- 0.12 for Standard MoE. Reference implementations (~200 lines each): https://github.com/russellwmy/affinity-is-not-enough

AI.tldr: Three lightweight gate modifications—temporal memory, precision-weighted gating, and anticipatory routing—solve Sparse MoE routing failures at domain transitions, achieving 124x improvement in correct expert assignment probability.
AI.motivation: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) routing fails dramatically at domain transitions, where current and next tokens belong to different distributions. Standard affinity routing performs poorly, assigning only 0.006 probability to the correct expert at transitions.
AI.method: Three gate modifications: 1) Temporal memory (beta) using per-expert LIF membrane potential to accumulate routing context; 2) Precision-weighted gating (Pi) using inverse variance of recent prediction error; 3) Anticipatory routing with next-state predictor conditioned on beta-accumulated hidden state. Inspired by Friston's Free Energy Principle and LIF dynamics from spiking neural networks.
AI.result: Combined modifications increase correct expert probability from 0.006 to 0.748 (124x improvement). Beta-routing reduces transition-step BPC from 6.56 to 4.01. Beta+Ant gate places 0.86 probability on correct expert before domain appears vs 0.42 for standard MoE. Super-additive interaction between beta and anticipation yields 75% of oracle gap.
AI.conclusion: The proposed gate modifications effectively solve MoE routing failures at domain transitions through temporal context accumulation and anticipation mechanisms, reducing needed experts for 99% coverage from infeasible to a small constant.

title: Decouple before Integration: Test-time Synthesis of SFT and RLVR Task Vectors
summary: SFT and RLVR represent two fundamental yet distinct paradigms for LLM post-training, each excelling in distinct dimensions. SFT expands knowledge breadth while RLVR enhances reasoning depth. Yet integrating these complementary strengths remains a formidable challenge. Sequential training can cause catastrophic forgetting, and joint optimization often suffers from severe gradient conflicts. We analyze SFT and RLVR through the lens of task vectors and reveal three structural properties behind these failures: a 30* magnitude disparity, 45* sign interference, and heterogeneous module-wise update distributions. These findings show SFT and RLVR are difficult to integrate directly, but they also suggest that the two paradigms modify partly complementary components of the model. Motivated by these observations, we propose Decoupled Test-time Synthesis (DoTS), a post-hoc framework allows SFT and RLVR checkpoints to be trained independently and synthesizes their capabilities only at inference time via task vector arithmetic, without updating model parameters. To reduce interference, DOTS applies selective sparsification with norm-preserving rescaling. It then uses Bayesian optimization on a small set of unlabeled queries to search for combination coefficients on the Pareto frontier of consistency and perplexity. Empirically, \ours matches or exceeds the performance of training-based SFT--RLVR integration methods across multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks, incurring only $\sim$3\% of the computational cost. When applied to stronger post-trained checkpoints, DOTS surpasses SOTA models and generalizes to out-of-domain benchmarks without re-tuning. Code is available at https://github.com/chaohaoyuan/DoTS.

AI.tldr: DoTS is a post-hoc framework that synthesizes SFT and RLVR capabilities at inference time without parameter updates, achieving comparable performance to training-based methods at 3% computational cost.
AI.motivation: SFT and RLVR are complementary LLM post-training paradigms (SFT for knowledge breadth, RLVR for reasoning depth), but integrating them is challenging due to catastrophic forgetting, gradient conflicts, and structural incompatibilities in task vectors.
AI.method: Proposes Decoupled Test-time Synthesis (DoTS): SFT and RLVR checkpoints are trained independently, then synthesized at inference via task vector arithmetic with selective sparsification and norm-preserving rescaling. Bayesian optimization searches combination coefficients on a small unlabeled query set.
AI.result: Matches/exceeds training-based SFT-RLVR integration methods across mathematical reasoning benchmarks with ~3% computational cost. Surpasses SOTA models when applied to stronger checkpoints and generalizes to out-of-domain benchmarks without re-tuning.
AI.conclusion: DoTS provides an efficient, parameter-free alternative to integrate complementary LLM capabilities, overcoming structural conflicts between SFT and RLVR through post-hoc synthesis at inference time.

title: Class Angular Distortion Index for Dimensionality Reduction
summary: Dimensionality reduction (DR) techniques are often characterized by whether they preserve global, high-level structures in the data or local, neighborhood structures. This distinction matters in visualization: global methods can obscure clusters while local methods can over-emphasize them. Yet, even when clusters appear distinct, their relative arrangement in the projection may be arbitrary or misleading, a common issue in techniques such as t-SNE and UMAP. Existing cluster quality metrics either only measure cluster separability or assume spherical, globular clusters in the original space. We introduce the Class Angular Distortion Index (CADI), a metric that uses internal angles among point triples to determine the faithfulness of cluster organization in a projection. We show cases on both real and synthetic data where existing cluster metrics fail, but CADI provides an interpretable result. Since it relies on computing angles, CADI is also differentiable, enabling optimization. We demonstrate this with a CADI-based DR technique.

AI.tldr: CADI (Class Angular Distortion Index) is a new metric for evaluating dimensionality reduction projections that assesses faithfulness of cluster organization using angular relationships, overcoming limitations of existing metrics that only measure separability or assume spherical clusters.
AI.motivation: Existing dimensionality reduction (DR) techniques often distort cluster arrangements in projections - global methods can obscure clusters while local methods can over-emphasize them. Current cluster quality metrics are inadequate: they either only measure separability or assume spherical clusters, failing to evaluate faithfulness of cluster organization.
AI.method: CADI uses internal angles among point triples to determine the faithfulness of cluster organization in a projection. It compares angular relationships between points in the original space and their projected counterparts to assess distortion of cluster structure.
AI.result: CADI provides interpretable results where existing cluster metrics fail, demonstrating its effectiveness on both real and synthetic data. CADI is also differentiable, enabling optimization, which is demonstrated through a CADI-based DR technique.
AI.conclusion: CADI addresses a critical gap in evaluating DR projections by providing a metric that assesses faithfulness of cluster organization rather than just separability. Its differentiable nature also enables optimization-based DR techniques that can explicitly preserve cluster structure.

title: Unlearning Offline Stochastic Multi-Armed Bandits
comment: First two authors made an equal contribution
summary: Machine unlearning aims to unlearn data points from a learned model, offering a principled way to process data-deletion requests and mitigate privacy risks without full retraining. Prior work has mainly studied unsupervised / supervised machine unlearning, leaving unlearning for sequential decision-making systems far less understood. We initiate the first study of a foundational sequential decision-making problem: offline stochastic multi-armed bandits (MAB). We formalize the privacy constraint for offline MAB and measure utility by the post-unlearning decision quality. We conduct a systematic study of both single- and multi-source unlearning scenarios under two data-generation models, the fixed-sample model and the distribution model. For these settings, our algorithmic design is built on two canonical base algorithms: Gaussian mechanism and rollback, and we propose adaptive algorithms that switch between them according to the data regime and privacy constraint. We further introduce a mixing procedure that elucidates the rationale behind these baselines. We provide performance guarantees across the above settings and establish lower bounds under both dataset models. Experiments validate the predicted tradeoffs and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods.

AI.tldr: This paper initiates the first study of machine unlearning for offline stochastic multi-armed bandits, proposing adaptive algorithms combining Gaussian mechanism and rollback approaches with performance guarantees and lower bounds.
AI.motivation: Prior work has mainly studied unsupervised/supervised machine unlearning, leaving sequential decision-making systems far less understood. The authors aim to address machine unlearning for a foundational sequential decision-making problem: offline stochastic multi-armed bandits.
AI.method: Formalized privacy constraint for offline MAB and measured utility by post-unlearning decision quality. Conducted systematic study of single- and multi-source unlearning scenarios under fixed-sample and distribution models. Built algorithmic design on two canonical base algorithms: Gaussian mechanism and rollback, proposing adaptive algorithms that switch between them according to data regime and privacy constraint. Introduced a mixing procedure that elucidates rationale behind these baselines.
AI.result: Provided performance guarantees across the above settings and established lower bounds under both dataset models. Experiments validated the predicted tradeoffs and demonstrated effectiveness of proposed methods.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully initiates systematic study of machine unlearning for offline stochastic multi-armed bandits, providing algorithmic solutions, theoretical guarantees, and practical validation for this important problem in sequential decision-making systems.

title: Knowing when to trust machine-learned interatomic potentials
summary: Prevailing machine-learned interatomic potential (MLIP) uncertainty-quantification methods rely on ensembles of independently trained backbones. These methods scale unfavorably with foundation-scale MLIPs, and their member-disagreement signals correlate weakly with per-molecule prediction error. Here we probe the frozen per-atom representations of a pretrained MLIP with a compact discriminative classifier, recasting MLIP uncertainty quantification as selective classification rather than error regression. The resulting method, PROBE (Post-hoc Reliability frOm Backbone Embeddings), produces a per-prediction reliability probability that monotonically tracks actual error without modification to the underlying model. Across large held-out evaluation sets and two structurally distinct MLIP architectures, PROBE outperforms ensemble disagreement as a binary reliability signal, which strengthens with the expressiveness of the backbone representation, implying a favorable scaling trajectory toward foundation-scale MLIPs. Multi-head self-attention additionally yields per-atom importance maps, providing chemically interpretable diagnostics at no additional computational cost. PROBE is post-hoc and architecture-agnostic, and is directly deployable on any MLIP that exposes per-atom representations.

AI.tldr: PROBE: a post-hoc method using a compact classifier on frozen MLIP embeddings for uncertainty quantification, outperforming ensembles and scaling well.
AI.motivation: Overcome limitations of ensemble-based MLIP uncertainty quantification (poor scaling, weak correlation with error) with a scalable, post-hoc approach.
AI.method: Probe frozen per-atom representations of a pretrained MLIP with a compact discriminative classifier, recasting uncertainty quantification as selective classification.
AI.result: PROBE outperforms ensemble disagreement as a binary reliability signal, with performance strengthening with backbone expressiveness, implying favorable scaling toward foundation-scale MLIPs.
AI.conclusion: PROBE offers a promising post-hoc, architecture-agnostic approach for uncertainty quantification in MLIPs that scales favorably and provides interpretable diagnostics.

title: Bridging Graph Drawing and Dimensionality Reduction with Stochastic Stress Optimization
summary: Both Dimensionality Reduction (DR) and Graph Drawing (GD) aim to visualize abstract, non-linear structures, yet rely on different optimization paradigms. This contrast is evident in Multidimensional Scaling (MDS), which typically depends on the SMACOF algorithm despite graph drawing results showing that simpler stochastic optimization schemes can be more effective for the same objective. We bridge these domains by adapting Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) techniques from graph drawing to vector data embedding. We present a scikit-learn compatible estimator that minimizes global stress through local pairwise updates, improving upon the existing implementation. Experiments on standard high-dimensional benchmarks show that our stochastic solver converges substantially faster than SMACOF while achieving comparable or lower stress.

AI.tldr: Adapting stochastic gradient descent from graph drawing to dimensionality reduction yields a scikit-learn compatible MDS estimator that converges faster than SMACOF with comparable or lower stress.
AI.motivation: Dimensionality Reduction (DR) and Graph Drawing (GD) both aim to visualize abstract, non-linear structures but use different optimization paradigms. While Multidimensional Scaling (MDS) relies on SMACOF, graph drawing results show that simpler stochastic optimization schemes can be more effective for the same objective.
AI.method: We present a scikit-learn compatible estimator that minimizes global stress through local pairwise updates, adapting Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) techniques from graph drawing to vector data embedding.
AI.result: Experiments on standard high-dimensional benchmarks show that our stochastic solver converges substantially faster than SMACOF while achieving comparable or lower stress.
AI.conclusion: Our stochastic solver converges substantially faster than SMACOF while achieving comparable or lower stress, suggesting that stochastic gradient descent methods can effectively bridge the gap between dimensionality reduction and graph drawing optimization paradigms.

title: From Prediction to Practice: A Task-Aware Evaluation Framework for Blood Glucose Forecasting
summary: Clinical time-series forecasting is increasingly studied for decision support, yet standard aggregate metrics can obscure whether a model is actually useful for the task it is meant to serve. In safety-critical settings, low average error can coexist with dangerous failures in exactly the high-risk regimes that matter most. We present a task-aware evaluation framework for blood glucose forecasting built around two downstream uses: hypoglycemia early warning and insulin dosing decision support. For early warning, we evaluate on real data from three clinical cohorts using event-level recall and false alarms per patient-day, metrics that reflect operational alarm burden rather than aggregate accuracy. We show that models appearing acceptable overall, with recall above 0.9 on the full test set, can fail badly in the post-bolus slice, where insulin-on-board is elevated and missed warnings carry the greatest clinical consequences. Standard forecasting evaluation, however, does not test whether a model can reason about the effects of actions, a requirement for supporting insulin dosing decisions. We therefore add a second, interventional arm using the FDA-accepted UVA/Padova simulator, where we evaluate whether forecasters can predict glucose responses to altered insulin plans in paired factual/counterfactual scenarios. We show that models that look strong on real-data forecasting often fail to predict the direction, magnitude, or ranking of intervention effects, and choose poor insulin doses when evaluated under a clinically motivated cost. Taken together, the two arms reveal a consistent gap between forecasting accuracy and task-relevant usefulness. We release the benchmark, the standardized preprocessing pipeline for public cohorts, and the simulator-based interventional dataset as a reproducible toolkit.

AI.tldr: A task-aware evaluation framework for blood glucose forecasting reveals that standard metrics mask dangerous failures in high-risk scenarios, showing models that look good overall perform poorly for critical clinical tasks like hypoglycemia warning and insulin dosing.
AI.motivation: Standard aggregate metrics in clinical time-series forecasting can obscure whether models are actually useful for their intended tasks, especially in safety-critical settings where low average error can coexist with dangerous failures in high-risk regimes.
AI.method: Develops a two-part evaluation framework: 1) Real-data evaluation using event-level recall and false alarms per patient-day for hypoglycemia early warning, and 2) Interventional evaluation using the UVA/Padova simulator to assess prediction of glucose responses to altered insulin plans in factual/counterfactual scenarios.
AI.result: Models that appear acceptable overall can fail badly in critical scenarios (e.g., post-bolus periods). Models strong on real-data forecasting often fail to predict intervention effects correctly and choose poor insulin doses. A consistent gap exists between forecasting accuracy and task-relevant usefulness.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a task-aware evaluation framework for clinical time-series forecasting that reveals significant gaps between standard forecasting accuracy metrics and task-relevant usefulness, particularly in safety-critical applications like blood glucose management.

title: PEACE: Cross-modal Enhanced Pediatric-Adult ECG Alignment for Robust Pediatric Diagnosis
summary: Automated pediatric electrocardiogram (ECG) diagnosis remains challenging because models trained predominantly on adult data suffer from substantial cross-population mismatch, while pediatric labels are often scarce. We present PEACE (Pediatric-Adult ECG Alignment via Cross-modal Enhancement), a structured cross-modal alignment framework for adult-to-pediatric ECG transfer. PEACE integrates tri-axial clinical semantic decomposition, label-query feature extraction, and curriculum-gated optimization to align transferable adult ECG representations with pediatric diagnostic targets. Since ZZU-pECG provides no paired clinical reports, we generate label-conditioned semantic descriptors using Gemini with concise clinical prompts and use them only as auxiliary training supervision; inference remains ECG-only. On ZZU-pECG, PEACE achieves 59.39%, 79.03%, and 90.89% AUC under zero-shot, 50-shot, and full fine-tuning settings, respectively, and reaches 96.65% AUC on the shared PTB-XL label space. These results suggest that structured clinical semantic supervision can improve low-resource adult-to-pediatric ECG transfer, while prospective clinical validation and more explicit age-aware modeling remain necessary before real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: PEACE framework uses cross-modal alignment to transfer adult ECG knowledge to pediatric diagnosis, leveraging clinical semantic supervision despite lack of paired reports, achieving high performance on pediatric ECG datasets.
AI.motivation: Pediatric ECG diagnosis is challenging due to data scarcity and cross-population mismatch between adult-trained models and pediatric data.
AI.method: PEACE integrates tri-axial clinical semantic decomposition, label-query feature extraction, and curriculum-gated optimization to align adult ECG representations with pediatric targets. Uses Gemini-generated semantic descriptors as auxiliary supervision.
AI.result: Achieves 59.39%, 79.03%, and 90.89% AUC on ZZU-pECG under zero-shot, 50-shot, and full fine-tuning; 96.65% AUC on PTB-XL label space.
AI.conclusion: Structured clinical semantic supervision improves low-resource adult-to-pediatric ECG transfer, but needs clinical validation and explicit age-aware modeling before real-world deployment.

title: Budget Constraints as Riemannian Manifolds
summary: Assigning one of K options to each of N groups under a total cost budget is a recurring problem in machine learning, appearing in mixed-precision quantization, non-uniform pruning, and expert selection. The objective (model loss) depends jointly on all assignments and does not decompose across groups, which prevents combinatorial solvers from optimizing the true objective directly and limits them to proxy objectives. Evolutionary search evaluates the actual loss but lacks gradient information, while penalty-based methods provide gradients but enforce the budget only approximately and require sensitive hyperparameter tuning. We observe that under softmax relaxation, the budget constraint defines a smooth Riemannian manifold in logit space with particularly simple geometry: the normal vector is available in closed form, shifting logits along the cost vector changes expected cost monotonically, allowing binary-search retraction, and vector transport reduces to a single inner product. Building on this structure, we propose Riemannian Constrained Optimization (RCO), which augments a standard Adam update with tangent projection, binary-search retraction, and momentum transport. Combined with Gumbel straight-through estimation and budget-constrained dynamic programming for discrete feasibility, RCO enables first-order optimization of the true objective under exact budget enforcement, without introducing constraint hyperparameters. On synthetic knapsack problems with known optima, the manifold-based constraint handling recovers optimal solutions, whereas penalty methods plateau at 83% of optimal. On LLM compression tasks, including mixed-precision quantization and MoE expert pruning, RCO matches or exceeds evolutionary search methods while requiring 3x to 16x lower wall-clock cost on the evaluated configurations.

AI.tldr: RCO is a Riemannian constrained optimization method that enables gradient-based optimization of discrete assignments under exact budget constraints, outperforming existing approaches in efficiency and solution quality.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for budget-constrained discrete optimization (e.g., mixed-precision quantization, pruning) either use proxy objectives, lack gradients, or enforce constraints only approximately with sensitive hyperparameters.
AI.method: RCO uses Riemannian manifold optimization with softmax relaxation, incorporating tangent projection, binary-search retraction, and momentum transport, along with Gumbel straight-through estimation and budget-constrained dynamic programming.
AI.result: RCO recovers optimal solutions on synthetic knapsack problems, outperforming penalty methods, and matches or exceeds evolutionary search on LLM compression tasks with 3x to 16x lower wall-clock cost.
AI.conclusion: RCO is a novel and effective method for constrained discrete optimization, offering a principled alternative to existing approaches by leveraging the geometric structure of budget constraints.

title: Evaluating the Architectural Reasoning Capabilities of LLM Provers via the Obfuscated Natural Number Game
comment: 4 pages. Accepted as a short paper to the AAAI 2026 Spring Symposium on Machine Learning and Knowledge Engineering for Knowledge-Grounded Semantic Agents (MAKE 2026)
summary: While Large Language Models have achieved notable success on formal mathematics benchmarks such as MiniF2F, it remains unclear whether these results stem from genuine logical reasoning or semantic pattern matching against pre-training data. This paper identifies Architectural Reasoning: the ability to synthesize formal proofs using exclusively local axioms and definitions within an alien math domain, as the necessary ability for future automated theorem discovery AI. We use the Obfuscated Natural Number Game, a benchmark to evaluate Architectural Reasoning. By renaming identifiers in the Natural Number Game in Lean 4, we created a zero-knowledge, closed environment. We evaluate state-of-the-art models, finding a universal latency tax where obfuscation increases inference time. The results also reveal a divergence in robustness: while general models (Claude-Sonnet-4.5, GPT-4o) suffer performance degradation, reasoning models (DeepSeek-R1, GPT-5, DeepSeek-Prover-V2) maintain the same accuracy despite the absence of semantic cues. These findings provide a quantitative metric for assessing the true capacity for mathematical reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Architectural Reasoning as the ability to prove theorems using only local axioms in an alien domain, evaluates it via the Obfuscated Natural Number Game, and finds reasoning models maintain accuracy under obfuscation while general models degrade, providing a metric for true mathematical reasoning.
AI.motivation: To determine whether Large Language Models' success on formal mathematics benchmarks stems from genuine logical reasoning or merely semantic pattern matching against pre-training data. The paper aims to identify and evaluate Architectural Reasoning as the necessary ability for future automated theorem discovery AI.
AI.method: The authors created the Obfuscated Natural Number Game benchmark by renaming identifiers in the Natural Number Game in Lean 4, producing a zero-knowledge, closed environment. They evaluated state-of-the-art models, measuring both inference time (latency tax) and accuracy under obfuscation, comparing general models (Claude-Sonnet-4.5, GPT-4o) against reasoning models (DeepSeek-R1, GPT-5, DeepSeek-Prover-V2).
AI.result: The results show a universal latency tax where obfuscation increases inference time for all models. More importantly, they reveal a divergence in robustness: general models suffer performance degradation under obfuscation, while reasoning models maintain the same accuracy despite the absence of semantic cues, indicating their capacity for genuine architectural reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Architectural Reasoning, defined as the ability to synthesize formal proofs using only local axioms and definitions in an alien math domain, is a crucial capability for future automated theorem discovery AI.
